class_schedule.topic: The Role of Finance in the Firm and Financial Markets Overview
learning_objectives: Explain the three fundamental decisions of corporate finance: investment (capital budgeting), financing, and working capital management
Distinguish between the goal of shareholder wealth maximization and alternative objectives such as profit maximization or stakeholder welfare
Describe the structure of financial markets including primary vs secondary markets, money vs capital markets, and exchange vs OTC markets
Identify the principal-agent problem between shareholders and managers and the mechanisms (compensation, boards, takeovers) that mitigate it
readings: Brealey, Myers & Allen, Principles of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 1 - Goals and Governance of the Corporation
Ross, Westerfield & Jordan, Fundamentals of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 1 - Introduction to Corporate Finance
Berk & DeMarzo, Corporate Finance (5th ed.), Ch. 1 - The Corporation and Financial Markets
Jensen & Meckling, 'Theory of the Firm: Managerial Behavior, Agency Costs and Ownership Structure' (1976)

lecture_notes.introduction: Every major decision a company makes eventually lands on the finance desk. When Apple decides whether to build a new chip fabrication partnership, when Tesla decides to issue $5 billion of new stock, when Boeing suspends its dividend during the 737 MAX crisis, or when Disney borrows $35 billion to acquire 21st Century Fox, what looks like a strategic or operational choice is ultimately a financial decision evaluated through the lens of whether it creates value for the owners of the firm. Business finance is the discipline that answers three deceptively simple questions: what long-term investments should the firm make (capital budgeting), how should the firm pay for those investments (capital structure), and how should the firm manage its day-to-day cash, inventory, receivables, and payables (working capital). The answers rest on a small set of unifying ideas: a dollar today is worth more than a dollar tomorrow, risk must be compensated with return, cash flows and not accounting profits determine value, and markets price securities based on the present value of their expected future cash flows. In this opening session we frame the course around these questions, introduce the players (firms, investors, intermediaries, regulators), tour the markets where securities trade, and confront the uncomfortable fact that the people running the firm are not the people who own it, creating the agency problem that lies at the heart of modern corporate governance.
key_concepts: The three corporate finance decisions: capital budgeting (which long-lived projects to accept), capital structure (the debt-equity mix used to finance the firm), and working capital management (managing current assets and liabilities to support daily operations)

Shareholder wealth maximization as the financial objective: the textbook goal is to maximize the market value of existing shareholders' equity per share, which in efficient markets equals the present value of expected future cash flows to shareholders

Separation of ownership and control: modern corporations have dispersed shareholders and professional managers; this separation is economically useful (specialization) but creates the agency problem described by Jensen and Meckling (1976)

Agency costs: monitoring costs (boards, auditors, analysts), bonding costs (executive compensation tied to stock, clawback clauses), and residual loss from imperfect alignment even after monitoring and bonding

Financial markets: primary markets (where firms issue new securities to raise capital, e.g., IPOs and seasoned equity offerings) vs secondary markets (where existing securities trade among investors, providing liquidity)
Money markets (short-term instruments under one year: T-bills, commercial paper, repo) vs capital markets (long-term instruments: bonds, stocks, mortgages); both are essential sources of financing
Financial intermediaries: commercial banks, investment banks, mutual funds, pension funds, hedge funds, and private equity funds channel capital from savers to firms and households

Forms of business organization: sole proprietorship (unlimited liability, pass-through tax), partnership, LLC, C-corporation (limited liability, separate tax entity, easier capital raising), S-corporation (pass-through), with corporations dominating public capital markets
Sarbanes-Oxley (2002) and Dodd-Frank (2010) regulatory frameworks: established after the Enron collapse and 2008 financial crisis to improve governance, disclosure, and systemic risk oversight

The efficient markets hypothesis in three forms (weak, semi-strong, strong) provides the baseline assumption that current security prices reflect available information, though empirical violations and behavioral anomalies moderate this view
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through Apple Inc.'s 2023 capital allocation as a live case study using the 10-K filing. Apple reported roughly $99.8 billion of operating cash flow, spent approximately $11 billion on property, plant and equipment, paid $15 billion in dividends, and repurchased about $77 billion of its own shares while carrying around $111 billion of long-term debt against roughly $62 billion of cash and marketable securities. Ask students: why does the most valuable company in the world deliberately carry tens of billions of debt while sitting on enormous cash? Why did Apple initiate dividends only in 2012 after decades without any, and why are buybacks roughly five times larger than dividends? Use this to preview capital budgeting, capital structure, and payout policy topics, showing that the course exists to explain what they just saw.
setup_requirements: Access to SEC EDGAR for 10-K and 10-Q filings of Apple, Tesla, Boeing, and Microsoft
Bloomberg Terminal or Yahoo Finance access for market data and historical prices
Financial calculator (TI BA II Plus or HP 10bII+) or Excel with financial functions
Brealey, Myers & Allen textbook with instructor-provided case packet

review.key: Why Shareholder Wealth, Not Accounting Profit
review.value: Accounting profit can be manipulated through depreciation methods, inventory assumptions, and accrual timing; shareholder wealth (stock price) reflects market-determined present value of expected future cash flows, is much harder to fake, and aligns with what owners actually receive when they sell their shares

Dispersed shareholders cannot directly run a multi-billion dollar firm, so they delegate to professional managers; this creates the agency problem whose solution is the entire corporate governance architecture of boards, compensation contracts, disclosure rules, and the market for corporate control

Firms only raise cash in the primary market (IPOs, seasoned offerings, bond issuances); the secondary market provides liquidity that makes the primary market possible because investors will only buy new issues if they know they can sell them later

Finance cares about cash flows, not accounting earnings, because only cash can be reinvested, used to pay dividends, or service debt; the entire course reduces to forecasting, discounting, and valuing cash flows under uncertainty

class_schedule.topic: Financial Statement Review and Financial Analysis for Finance
learning_objectives: Reconstruct the three major financial statements (income statement, balance sheet, cash flow statement) and explain how they articulate
Compute and interpret the major categories of financial ratios: liquidity, leverage, efficiency, profitability, and market value ratios
Apply DuPont decomposition to isolate the drivers of return on equity into operating efficiency, asset utilization, and financial leverage
Identify the common manipulations and red flags in financial statements (channel stuffing, capitalized operating expenses, aggressive revenue recognition)
readings: Brealey, Myers & Allen, Principles of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 28 - Financial Analysis
Ross, Westerfield & Jordan, Fundamentals of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 3 - Working with Financial Statements
Berk & DeMarzo, Corporate Finance (5th ed.), Ch. 2 - Introduction to Financial Statement Analysis
Penman, Financial Statement Analysis and Security Valuation (5th ed.), Ch. 7-9

lecture_notes.introduction: Before you can value a company, recommend a financing decision, or evaluate a project, you need to understand the numbers. The financial statements are the finance practitioner's raw material, and ratio analysis is how we transform pages of reported data into a coherent diagnosis of a firm's health. Consider General Electric: in 2017 the company reported $122 billion in revenue and looked, on the surface, like a diversified industrial powerhouse. But a careful reader of the financial statements would have seen the warning signs long before the stock collapsed from $30 to $6: pension underfunding, aggressive assumptions in the long-term service agreements of GE Power, operating cash flow well below reported earnings, and a debt load that looked manageable only under optimistic capital cost assumptions. By 2021, GE announced it would break itself into three pieces. The tools in this session, common-size statements, ratio categories, DuPont decomposition, and earnings quality red flags, are the same tools a credit analyst at Moody's, an equity analyst at Goldman Sachs, or a private equity associate uses every day. This is also where the prerequisite from BA102 Financial Accounting becomes a finance skill: accounting taught you how the statements are built, and this session teaches you how to interrogate them for decisions about value, risk, and strategy. By the end of this session you will be able to look at a 10-K and form a defensible view on whether the firm is solvent, profitable, efficient, and correctly valued relative to peers.
key_concepts: Income statement articulation: revenue minus cost of goods sold equals gross profit, minus operating expenses equals operating income (EBIT), minus interest equals pretax income, minus taxes equals net income; each subtotal matters for different ratio categories

Balance sheet identity: Assets = Liabilities + Stockholders' Equity; a snapshot at a point in time; book values generally reflect historical cost, not current market value, which is a core limitation when using balance sheets in valuation
Cash flow statement: reconciles net income to the change in cash through operating (CFO), investing (CFI), and financing (CFF) activities; the only statement that reports actual cash movement
Liquidity ratios: current ratio (current assets / current liabilities), quick ratio ((current assets - inventory) / current liabilities), cash ratio; measure ability to meet short-term obligations

Leverage ratios: debt-to-equity (total debt / equity), debt-to-assets, interest coverage (EBIT / interest expense), times interest earned; measure the firm's use of borrowed capital and ability to service debt

Efficiency ratios: inventory turnover (COGS / inventory), receivables turnover, asset turnover (sales / total assets), days sales outstanding, days inventory outstanding; measure how effectively assets are being used to generate sales

Profitability ratios: gross margin, operating margin, net margin, ROA (net income / assets), ROE (net income / equity), ROIC (NOPAT / invested capital); measure the ability to generate profit from sales, assets, and invested capital
Market value ratios: P/E, P/B, EV/EBITDA, dividend yield; link financial statements to market-determined prices and are the primary tools of relative valuation

DuPont decomposition: ROE = Net Profit Margin x Asset Turnover x Equity Multiplier = (Net Income / Sales) x (Sales / Assets) x (Assets / Equity); isolates whether ROE comes from pricing power, efficiency, or leverage

Earnings quality red flags: operating cash flow persistently below net income, large increases in receivables relative to sales, capitalized expenses, one-time gains reported as ordinary, aggressive revenue recognition, and off-balance-sheet financing arrangements
lecture_notes.demonstration: Construct a live DuPont analysis for Walmart vs Costco vs Target using their most recent 10-K filings. For Walmart (FY2023, approximate figures): Net income $11.7B, Sales $611B, Assets $243B, Equity $83B. Net margin = 11.7/611 = 1.9%, Asset turnover = 611/243 = 2.51, Equity multiplier = 243/83 = 2.93, ROE = 1.9% x 2.51 x 2.93 = 14.0%. For Costco: Net margin around 2.6%, asset turnover around 3.4, equity multiplier around 3.0, ROE around 26%. For Target: margins, turnover, and leverage compute to a different mix. Discuss: Walmart wins on asset turnover, Costco on the combination of margins and turnover, and Target often relies more on leverage. Use this to show that the same ROE can come from very different operating realities, and that retail investors who miss the decomposition can mistakenly conclude that firms with similar ROE have similar fundamentals.
setup_requirements: Excel worksheet template for common-size financial statements and ratio calculations
Most recent 10-K filings for Walmart, Costco, Target, and Amazon downloaded from SEC EDGAR
Ratio cheat sheet with formulas for liquidity, leverage, efficiency, profitability, and market value ratios
Bloomberg or Capital IQ terminal access for peer-group comparables

review.value: Net income can be distorted by accounting choices; operating cash flow is harder to manipulate; persistent divergence between earnings and CFO is one of the clearest warning signs of aggressive accounting, and was present in Enron, WorldCom, and more recently in the pre-collapse numbers at firms like Wirecard

review.key: DuPont Tells You Where ROE Comes From
review.value: Two firms with 20% ROE can be completely different: one might earn it through high margins on differentiated products (Apple-style), another through efficient asset use with thin margins (Costco-style), and a third through high leverage (a bank or a levered industrial); the investment implications and risk profiles are very different

A current ratio of 1.2 is scary for a manufacturing firm but normal for a fast-inventory retailer; always compare ratios to industry peers and to the firm's own historical trend, not to generic rules of thumb

Operating lease disclosures, pension assumptions, contingent liabilities, and variable interest entities often hide more economic risk than the balance sheet admits; GE's pension and long-term service agreement assumptions are a classic example where footnotes told a more honest story than headline ratios

class_schedule.topic: Time Value of Money I: PV, FV, Single Cash Flows
learning_objectives: Calculate future value and present value of single cash flows using both formulas and financial calculators
Apply the compounding principle to handle multiple compounding periods per year and continuous compounding
Solve for any missing variable (PV, FV, r, n) given the other three in the single cash flow equation
Explain why time value of money is the foundation of all valuation in finance and why ignoring it produces systematic mispricing
readings: Brealey, Myers & Allen, Principles of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 2 - How to Calculate Present Values
Ross, Westerfield & Jordan, Fundamentals of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 5 - Introduction to Valuation: The Time Value of Money
Berk & DeMarzo, Corporate Finance (5th ed.), Ch. 4 - The Time Value of Money
Damodaran, Corporate Finance: Theory and Practice (2nd ed.), Ch. 3

lecture_notes.introduction: Time value of money is the single most important concept in finance, and everything else in this course is essentially an application of it. A dollar today is worth more than a dollar next year because you can invest today's dollar to earn interest, because inflation erodes the purchasing power of tomorrow's dollar, and because the future is uncertain while the present is not. When Warren Buffett explains that intrinsic value is the discounted value of the cash that can be taken out of a business during its remaining life, he is invoking time value of money. When a bond trader quotes a yield to maturity, when an investment banker prices an LBO, when a CFO evaluates whether to build a new plant, they are all solving the same equation: what is the present value of an expected future cash flow, given an appropriate discount rate? This session establishes the core mechanics: the future value formula FV = PV(1 + r)^n, the present value formula PV = FV / (1 + r)^n, and their rearrangements to solve for the interest rate or the number of periods. We cover compounding frequency, because a 12% annual rate compounded monthly is not the same as 12% compounded annually, and we introduce the effective annual rate (EAR) to compare rates with different compounding conventions on equal footing. Once you truly believe that $1,000 today and $1,100 next year are not the same thing, and that ignoring this costs real money, the rest of finance unfolds as a set of increasingly sophisticated applications of this one idea.
key_concepts: Future value formula: FV = PV x (1 + r)^n; a dollar invested today at rate r grows to (1+r)^n dollars after n periods due to compounding
Present value formula: PV = FV / (1 + r)^n; the present value of a future dollar is what you would pay today to receive that dollar in the future, given the opportunity cost captured in r

Compounding: interest earning interest; $100 at 10% for 2 years grows to $121 (not $120) because year-two interest is computed on the $110 balance, and over long horizons this effect dominates simple interest
Rule of 72: a quick approximation for doubling time, n = 72/r (where r is a percentage); $1 at 8% doubles in approximately 9 years, useful for sanity checks without a calculator
Compounding frequency: m periods per year with nominal annual rate r gives FV = PV x (1 + r/m)^(m x n); as m approaches infinity we get continuous compounding FV = PV x e^(r x n)
Effective annual rate (EAR): EAR = (1 + r/m)^m - 1; converts a nominal rate with any compounding frequency into the equivalent annual rate, enabling apples-to-apples comparison of rates

Stated (APR) vs effective rates: a 24% APR with monthly compounding has an EAR of (1 + 0.24/12)^12 - 1 = 26.82%, which is why credit card disclosures highlight APR while the true cost to borrowers is the EAR
Solving for r: given PV, FV, and n, the implied rate is r = (FV/PV)^(1/n) - 1; this is how holding-period returns are converted to compound annual growth rates (CAGR)
Solving for n: given PV, FV, and r, the required number of periods is n = ln(FV/PV) / ln(1 + r); answers questions like 'how long until my investment doubles at 6%'

The discount rate reflects opportunity cost and risk: for a riskless cash flow it is the Treasury yield for the matching maturity, for a risky cash flow it is the Treasury yield plus an appropriate risk premium
lecture_notes.demonstration: Work three calculations live at the whiteboard. (1) If you invest $10,000 at 7% for 30 years, what is it worth? FV = 10000 x (1.07)^30 = 10000 x 7.6123 = $76,123, showing the power of long-horizon compounding. (2) You want $1,000,000 in 40 years for retirement; you can earn 8% annually; how much do you need to invest today? PV = 1,000,000 / (1.08)^40 = 1,000,000 / 21.7245 = $46,031. Illustrate how a small sum invested early can become a large retirement nest egg. (3) A $10,000 investment grew to $45,000 over 15 years; what was the annual return? r = (45000/10000)^(1/15) - 1 = 4.5^0.0667 - 1 = 1.1055 - 1 = 10.55% per year. Do each on both the TI BA II Plus (N, I/Y, PV, FV keys) and in Excel (=FV, =PV, =RATE), demonstrating two equivalent ways to solve.
setup_requirements: TI BA II Plus or HP 10bII+ financial calculator for each student
Excel spreadsheet with =PV, =FV, =RATE, =NPER function templates
Time value of money reference table with factors for common rates and periods
Worksheet of 20 practice problems spanning single cash flow scenarios

review.value: At 10% for 10 years, $1 becomes $2.59, not $2.00; at 10% for 30 years, $1 becomes $17.45, not $4.00; the nonlinearity is why starting to save early is vastly more valuable than starting later with larger amounts

A nominal rate of 12% compounded monthly has an EAR of (1.01)^12 - 1 = 12.68%; always convert to EAR before comparing rates from products with different compounding frequencies, or you will make systematically biased decisions

When you compute a present value, the discount rate should reflect the return you could earn on an investment of comparable risk; using the wrong discount rate, not the cash flow forecast, is often the dominant source of valuation error

review.key: Same Formula Solves All Four Variables
review.value: PV, FV, r, and n are linked by one equation; given any three, the fourth is determined, which is why financial calculators have four keys for these variables and one button to compute the missing one

class_schedule.topic: Time Value of Money II: Annuities, Perpetuities, and Uneven Cash Flows
learning_objectives: Value ordinary annuities and annuities due using closed-form formulas and financial calculators
Value perpetuities and growing perpetuities using the Gordon growth formula
Decompose and value streams of uneven cash flows using the general present value equation
Apply annuity techniques to real-world problems including mortgages, auto loans, retirement planning, and bond coupon streams
readings: Brealey, Myers & Allen, Principles of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 2 - How to Calculate Present Values (annuity sections)
Ross, Westerfield & Jordan, Fundamentals of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 6 - Discounted Cash Flow Valuation
Berk & DeMarzo, Corporate Finance (5th ed.), Ch. 4 - Time Value of Money (annuities and perpetuities)
Damodaran, Investment Valuation (3rd ed.), Ch. 3 - Understanding Financial Statements

lecture_notes.introduction: Most real-world cash flow streams are not single future payments; they are sequences: monthly mortgage payments, quarterly bond coupons, annual retirement withdrawals, uneven project cash flows. The annuity and perpetuity formulas collapse what would otherwise be tedious sums into a few lines of arithmetic, and without them the analysis of mortgages, pension obligations, insurance contracts, and bond portfolios would be impractical. When a homeowner signs a 30-year mortgage for $400,000 at 6.5%, the monthly payment of $2,528.27 is not computed term by term; it comes from inverting the ordinary annuity PV formula. When an actuary at a pension fund values the $80 billion of future benefits owed to retirees, the calculation is a massive present value of an annuity-like stream. When Apple buys a $5 billion data center with cash flows over 20 years, the NPV is the present value of an uneven series. In this session we derive the four core annuity formulas, show how to use financial calculators and Excel functions to avoid the algebra, and apply them to real problems. We also introduce the Gordon growth formula PV = C / (r - g), which values a perpetual stream growing at constant rate g and is the backbone of the dividend discount model for stocks. By the end of this session, you should be able to price any predictable stream of future cash flows: mortgages, bonds, preferred stock, dividends, annuity contracts, and pension obligations.
key_concepts: Ordinary annuity: a stream of equal cash flows C at the end of each period for n periods; PV = C x [1 - (1+r)^(-n)] / r; FV = C x [(1+r)^n - 1] / r
Annuity due: cash flows at the beginning of each period instead of the end; PV(due) = PV(ordinary) x (1+r); used for rental leases, insurance premiums, and some retirement distributions
Perpetuity: a level cash flow that continues forever; PV = C / r; British consols were the historical example, and perpetuity logic is used to value preferred stock and zero-growth businesses

Growing perpetuity (Gordon growth): cash flow grows at constant rate g forever; PV = C1 / (r - g), where C1 is next period's cash flow; requires r > g or the formula diverges, a mathematical reminder that no cash flow can grow faster than the discount rate forever
Growing annuity: a finite stream of cash flows growing at rate g for n periods; PV = (C1 / (r - g)) x [1 - ((1+g)/(1+r))^n]; used for projects with predictable growth over a finite horizon

Uneven cash flows: valued as the sum of individual PVs; PV = sum over t from 1 to n of CF_t / (1+r)^t; handled efficiently using the CF (cash flow) register on a financial calculator or the =NPV function in Excel

Mortgage amortization: each monthly payment has an interest portion (based on beginning balance) and a principal portion (payment minus interest); the schedule shows how early payments are mostly interest and later payments are mostly principal, a pattern essential for understanding refinancing decisions
Deferred annuities: payments begin after a delay; value the annuity as of one period before the first payment, then discount that value back to today, a two-step approach that avoids errors

The annuity formula as a geometric series: derivable from summing (1+r)^(-t) for t = 1 to n, which is why the formula shape is a closed-form geometric sum; understanding the derivation prevents the common mistake of misapplying the formula
Excel functions: =PV(rate, nper, pmt, [fv], [type]), =FV, =PMT, =NPV, =RATE; the TI BA II Plus has equivalent keys with N, I/Y, PV, PMT, FV, and a CF register for uneven streams
lecture_notes.demonstration: Solve a live mortgage problem using an annuity. A $400,000 mortgage at 6.5% APR for 30 years with monthly payments. Monthly rate = 0.065/12 = 0.005417, n = 360. PMT = 400,000 / [(1 - (1.005417)^(-360)) / 0.005417] = 400,000 / 158.2108 = $2,528.27 per month. Total payments over 30 years = $2,528.27 x 360 = $910,177, of which $510,177 is interest. Then solve for how much principal is paid in month 1 ($2,528.27 - 400,000 x 0.005417 = $2,528.27 - $2,166.67 = $361.60) vs month 300 (mostly principal, very little interest). Next, a retirement problem: a 25-year-old who saves $500 per month at 8% expected return for 40 years; FV = 500 x [(1.00667)^480 - 1] / 0.00667 = 500 x 3,491 = $1,745,000 at age 65. Compare to starting at age 35 instead of 25: FV = 500 x [(1.00667)^360 - 1] / 0.00667 = 500 x 1,491 = $745,500, less than half, even though they only contributed ten years less.
setup_requirements: Financial calculator with annuity keys (PMT, N, I/Y) and CF (cash flow) register
Excel amortization schedule template for 30-year mortgage
Retirement planning worksheet comparing early vs late savers
Real mortgage rate quotes from Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Market Survey

review.value: PV = C / r is the simplest valuation formula in finance and underlies the Gordon growth model, the terminal value in DCF valuations, and the quick-and-dirty valuation of stable cash flow businesses; its simplicity is also dangerous if applied to firms that are not actually stable

The Gordon formula C1/(r-g) diverges if g >= r, which is finance's way of saying no firm can sustainably grow its cash flows faster than its cost of capital forever; when analysts assume 6% growth and use an 8% discount rate, the resulting valuations are extremely sensitive to that 2% gap

In month 1 of a 30-year mortgage, about 86% of the payment is interest; in month 240, it is closer to 30%; this is why homeowners who refinance into a new 30-year loan reset the clock and pay far more interest over the life of the loan, even if the rate is slightly lower

The only difference is timing (end vs beginning of period), but over long horizons the effect compounds; an annuity due is worth (1+r) times an ordinary annuity, so a pension that pays at the start of each month is worth more than one that pays at the end

class_schedule.topic: Bond Valuation: Pricing, YTM, and Interest Rate Risk
learning_objectives: Price coupon bonds as the present value of coupon payments plus the present value of face value
Compute yield to maturity (YTM) from a given bond price and interpret YTM as the bond's internal rate of return
Explain the inverse relationship between bond prices and interest rates and quantify interest rate risk using duration
Distinguish between nominal yield, current yield, yield to maturity, and yield to call, and use each in the appropriate context
readings: Brealey, Myers & Allen, Principles of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 3 - Valuing Bonds
Ross, Westerfield & Jordan, Fundamentals of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 7 - Interest Rates and Bond Valuation
Berk & DeMarzo, Corporate Finance (5th ed.), Ch. 6 - Valuing Bonds
Fabozzi, Bond Markets, Analysis, and Strategies (10th ed.), Ch. 2-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Bonds are the largest asset class in the world. The U.S. Treasury market alone exceeds $25 trillion in outstanding debt, the global corporate bond market is over $40 trillion, and virtually every large company finances itself in part by issuing bonds. When Microsoft raised $17 billion in a single 2020 bond issuance to fund share repurchases and general corporate purposes, every one of those bonds was priced using the techniques in this session. When interest rates jumped in 2022, the price of long-duration Treasury bonds fell by more than 30%, wiping out a decade of returns in months, and this loss was directly predictable from the duration formula we will derive today. Bond valuation is conceptually simple: a bond is just a set of promised cash flows, the coupon payments plus the face value at maturity, and its price is the present value of those cash flows at the market-required yield. But the simplicity hides crucial subtleties: a bond's yield to maturity is not the same as its coupon rate, and only equals the coupon rate when the bond trades at par; bond prices and interest rates move inversely, which is why Silicon Valley Bank collapsed in 2023 when the bonds on its balance sheet lost value as rates rose; and the sensitivity of a bond's price to rate changes is measured by duration, which depends on the bond's coupon, maturity, and yield. This session gives you the tools to price any investment-grade bond, interpret the yield curve, and understand why a 30-year Treasury behaves nothing like a 2-year Treasury when rates move.
key_concepts: Bond structure: face value (typically $1,000), coupon rate (annual, paid semi-annually in the U.S.), maturity date, and the resulting coupon payment = face value x coupon rate / 2 for semi-annual bonds
Bond price formula: P = sum from t=1 to n of C/(1+r)^t + F/(1+r)^n, where C is the coupon payment, F is face value, r is the market yield per period, and n is the number of periods to maturity

Yield to maturity (YTM): the single discount rate that equates the present value of a bond's cash flows to its current market price; YTM is the IRR of the bond and the return earned if held to maturity assuming reinvestment at YTM
Par, premium, discount: when coupon rate = YTM the bond trades at par ($1,000); when coupon > YTM the bond trades at a premium (price > $1,000); when coupon < YTM the bond trades at a discount

Inverse price-yield relationship: when market yields rise, bond prices fall, and vice versa; this is arithmetic, not opinion, because discounting a fixed cash flow stream at a higher rate produces a lower present value
Current yield: annual coupon / current price; a rough yield measure that ignores capital gain or loss as the bond approaches par at maturity

Yield to call (YTC): for callable bonds, the yield assuming the bond is called at the earliest call date at the call price; investors in premium bonds compute YTC to avoid overpaying based on YTM when a call is likely
Macaulay duration: weighted average time to receive the bond's cash flows, weights being each cash flow's PV share of total PV; measures the effective maturity accounting for the coupon structure

Modified duration: Macaulay duration / (1 + y/m); approximates the percentage price change for a 1% change in yield; a bond with modified duration 8 loses approximately 8% of its price when yields rise 1%

Interest rate risk vs reinvestment risk: rising rates lower bond prices (bad for holders) but raise reinvestment income on coupons (good); duration matches the horizon at which these two effects offset, the basis of immunization strategies
lecture_notes.demonstration: Price a bond step by step. A 10-year bond with $1,000 face value, 5% annual coupon paid semi-annually, and YTM 6% (so 3% per period). Coupon payment = $25 per six months. Number of periods = 20. Price = sum from t=1 to 20 of 25/(1.03)^t + 1000/(1.03)^20. The annuity factor for 20 periods at 3% is [1 - (1.03)^(-20)] / 0.03 = 14.8775, so the coupon PV = 25 x 14.8775 = $371.94. The face value PV = 1000 / (1.03)^20 = 1000 / 1.8061 = $553.68. Price = 371.94 + 553.68 = $925.62. Below par because the coupon rate (5%) is below the YTM (6%). Now show what happens if YTM falls to 4%: coupon PV = 25 x 16.3514 = $408.79, face PV = 1000/(1.02)^20 = 1000/1.4859 = $672.97, new price = $1,081.76. A 2-percentage-point drop in yield moved the price from $925.62 to $1,081.76, a 16.9% gain, illustrating the convex, amplified price response of long-duration bonds to rate moves.
setup_requirements: Financial calculator configured with P/Y = 2 for semi-annual bonds
Excel template with =PRICE and =YIELD functions and bond pricing worksheet
Current Treasury yield curve data from the U.S. Treasury Department website
Sample corporate bond indentures showing call provisions and coupon schedules

review.value: The coupon rate is fixed in the indenture and never changes; the yield to maturity changes every minute as market prices move; the coupon determines the cash flows but the yield determines the return, and they only coincide when the bond trades at par

A 30-year bond has much higher duration than a 2-year bond, so a 1% change in rates moves its price by roughly 15% vs 2%; this is why long-term Treasury bond funds lost more than 30% in 2022 while short-term funds lost only a few percent

A callable bond gives the issuer the right to refinance at lower rates, so when rates fall the bond's price rises only to near the call price, not above; investors should compute yield to worst (minimum of YTM and YTC) and use that for decisions

review.key: Interest Rate Risk Is Unavoidable in Bonds
review.value: Even default-free Treasury bonds have interest rate risk; the only way to avoid it is to hold a zero-coupon bond to the exact date you need the money, matching duration to investment horizon, which is the basis of liability-driven investing in pension funds

class_schedule.topic: Stock Valuation: DDM, Growth Models, and Relative Valuation
learning_objectives: Value stocks using the dividend discount model (DDM) under zero-growth, constant-growth, and multi-stage assumptions
Apply relative valuation using P/E, EV/EBITDA, P/B, and P/S multiples with appropriate peer selection and adjustments
Decompose P/E into its fundamental drivers using the Gordon growth formula and identify when a high P/E is justified
Explain the theoretical and practical limitations of each valuation approach and choose the appropriate method for a given firm
readings: Brealey, Myers & Allen, Principles of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 4 - The Value of Common Stocks
Ross, Westerfield & Jordan, Fundamentals of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 8 - Stock Valuation
Berk & DeMarzo, Corporate Finance (5th ed.), Ch. 9 - Valuing Stocks
Damodaran, Investment Valuation (3rd ed.), Ch. 13-18 - Dividend Discount Models and Relative Valuation

lecture_notes.introduction: A share of stock is a claim on the future cash flows of a business, and in the simplest theoretical framing those cash flows are the dividends paid to shareholders plus any terminal selling price. The dividend discount model (DDM) takes this logic and prices the stock as the present value of all future dividends, which collapses into the elegant Gordon growth formula P = D1 / (r - g) for a firm paying a dividend growing at a constant rate forever. But most real-world stocks are not valued this way in practice, because many firms (Amazon until 2024, Berkshire Hathaway still) pay no dividends, and growth is rarely constant. So analysts supplement DDM with relative valuation: pricing stocks based on multiples like P/E, EV/EBITDA, and P/B applied to comparable companies. When Tesla trades at 70x earnings while Ford trades at 7x, the question is not which multiple is wrong but what each multiple implies about expected growth, required return, and profitability. In 2003, Microsoft initiated its first dividend (a special dividend of $3 per share plus a 16 cent quarterly), and analysts immediately rewrote their DDM models, recognizing that Microsoft's maturing cash generation justified returning cash to shareholders. In 2024, Alphabet and Meta did the same thing, a structural shift in the tech sector from pure reinvestment to capital return. This session builds the tools to handle all these cases: constant growth for mature firms, multi-stage DDM for transitioning firms, free cash flow to equity for non-dividend payers, and multiples for quick cross-sectional comparisons.
key_concepts: Dividend discount model (DDM): P0 = sum from t=1 to infinity of D_t / (1+r)^t; the price today equals the present value of all expected future dividends using the required return r
Zero-growth DDM: P0 = D / r; a perpetuity model suitable for preferred stock and mature firms with flat dividends
Constant-growth DDM (Gordon): P0 = D1 / (r - g); requires r > g, applies to mature firms with stable growth, and is extremely sensitive to the assumed growth rate

Multi-stage DDM: different growth rates for different phases of a firm's life cycle; typical two-stage model has a high growth phase for 5-10 years followed by a stable phase, with the stable phase value computed as a Gordon terminal value and discounted back
Required return r for equity: can be estimated using CAPM (coming in session 8) or the dividend yield plus growth decomposition r = D1/P0 + g; the latter rearranges the Gordon formula
Sustainable growth rate: g = ROE x (1 - payout ratio) = ROE x retention rate; tells you the maximum growth a firm can sustain using only retained earnings without external financing

Free cash flow to equity (FCFE) valuation: for firms without steady dividends, value the present value of cash flows available to equity holders after capital expenditures, debt service, and working capital needs

P/E ratio drivers: P/E = payout / (r - g), derived by dividing Gordon by earnings; a high P/E is justified by high growth, low required return, or high payout efficiency, and a low P/E by the opposite
EV/EBITDA multiple: enterprise value divided by EBITDA; commonly used in M&A because it is capital-structure neutral (EV includes debt) and less distorted by depreciation and tax differences

Peer selection and adjustments: the hardest practical step; comparables must share growth, risk, and capital intensity characteristics, and adjustments are often needed for differences in leverage, one-time items, and accounting practices
lecture_notes.demonstration: Value a stock three ways and compare. Consider a mature utility company with current dividend $2.00, expected growth 5%, required return 8%. Constant-growth DDM: P = 2.00 x 1.05 / (0.08 - 0.05) = 2.10 / 0.03 = $70.00. Now value a firm with next year's dividend $1.50, growing at 15% for 5 years, then 4% thereafter, with required return 10%. High-growth dividends: $1.50, $1.725, $1.984, $2.281, $2.623 in years 1 through 5. Terminal value at end of year 5 = 2.623 x 1.04 / (0.10 - 0.04) = 2.728 / 0.06 = $45.47. Discount each to present: 1.50/1.10 + 1.725/1.21 + 1.984/1.331 + 2.281/1.4641 + (2.623 + 45.47)/1.6105 = 1.364 + 1.426 + 1.491 + 1.558 + 29.86 = $35.70. Third, use a P/E multiple: if peer P/E is 18 and forward EPS is $3.00, P = 18 x 3 = $54. Discuss which valuation to trust: if all three cluster you have confidence, if they diverge you must investigate assumptions.
setup_requirements: Excel DDM template with single-stage and two-stage tabs
Recent analyst research reports from Morgan Stanley or Goldman Sachs for model comparison
Bloomberg Terminal access for peer comparable screening
Value Line or Morningstar reports showing dividend history and growth assumptions

review.value: With r = 8% and g = 5%, the price is D1/0.03; with r = 8% and g = 6%, the price is D1/0.02, 50% higher; this is why small changes in growth assumptions move valuations dramatically, and why analysts should stress-test g ranges rather than report point estimates

Amazon paid no dividends from its 1997 IPO until its first-ever dividend announced in 2024, yet its value grew to trillions; for non-dividend firms, use FCFE or FCFF models, or rely on relative valuation, because the DDM predicts price = 0 for any firm that never pays dividends

A P/E of 25 is not inherently high or low; it represents an implicit growth-and-risk assumption; decompose it using the Gordon-derived P/E formula and you can test whether the implicit assumptions are plausible, which is how disciplined value investors separate expensive quality from overpriced hype

A firm can only grow faster than g = ROE x retention ratio by issuing equity or debt, which eventually hits a limit; growth projections that ignore this constraint are financially inconsistent, a frequent error in bull-case DCF models

class_schedule.topic: Risk and Return: Variance, Covariance, and Diversification
learning_objectives: Compute expected return, variance, and standard deviation for a single asset and a portfolio of assets
Calculate portfolio variance using covariance and correlation, and decompose total risk into systematic and idiosyncratic components
Construct the efficient frontier from a set of risky assets and identify the minimum variance portfolio
Explain why diversification reduces risk without reducing expected return and quantify the diversification benefit as correlation varies
readings: Brealey, Myers & Allen, Principles of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 7-8 - Introduction to Risk, Return, and Portfolio Theory
Ross, Westerfield & Jordan, Fundamentals of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 11 - Risk and Return
Berk & DeMarzo, Corporate Finance (5th ed.), Ch. 10-11 - Capital Markets and the Pricing of Risk, Portfolio Choice
Markowitz, 'Portfolio Selection' (1952), Journal of Finance

lecture_notes.introduction: In 1952 Harry Markowitz published a 14-page paper that earned him the Nobel Prize and founded modern portfolio theory. His insight was that investors care about the risk and return of their entire portfolio, not of each individual asset, and that by combining imperfectly correlated assets, a portfolio can achieve lower risk than any of its components without sacrificing expected return. This is the closest thing to a free lunch in finance, and it fundamentally changed how institutional investors allocate capital. Before Markowitz, investment advice was asset-by-asset: buy good stocks, avoid bad ones. After Markowitz, it became portfolio-level: optimize the combination. Today, every pension fund, endowment, hedge fund, and target-date retirement fund uses some version of mean-variance optimization, and the concepts we cover today are the building blocks. We define expected return as a probability-weighted average of possible outcomes, variance as the expected squared deviation from the mean, and standard deviation as the square root of variance (a measure of typical dispersion). We then move to portfolios: the expected return of a portfolio is a weighted average of component returns, but the variance is not; it depends on covariances between pairs of assets, and this is where diversification works its magic. A portfolio of 30 stocks across different industries has dramatically lower risk than a single stock with the same expected return, because the idiosyncratic shocks that hit individual firms partially cancel out. This session prepares you for the CAPM, which will explain how the market prices the risk that cannot be diversified away.
key_concepts: Expected return E(R) = sum of p_i x R_i, where p_i is the probability of state i and R_i is the return in that state; for historical data, the arithmetic mean of past returns is used as an estimate
Variance Var(R) = E[(R - E(R))^2] = sum of p_i x (R_i - E(R))^2; measures dispersion, with units of squared returns
Standard deviation sigma(R) = sqrt(Var(R)); in the same units as returns (percent per period), so it is the standard measure of risk in finance
Portfolio expected return: E(R_p) = sum of w_i x E(R_i); a weighted average where weights are the fraction of the portfolio in each asset
Covariance Cov(R_i, R_j) = E[(R_i - E(R_i))(R_j - E(R_j))]; measures how two returns co-move, positive when they tend to move together, negative when they move in opposite directions

Correlation rho = Cov(R_i, R_j) / (sigma_i x sigma_j); a standardized measure between -1 and +1, where +1 means perfectly positively correlated, 0 means uncorrelated, and -1 means perfectly negatively correlated
Two-asset portfolio variance: Var(R_p) = w1^2 x sigma1^2 + w2^2 x sigma2^2 + 2 x w1 x w2 x rho x sigma1 x sigma2; the cross term is the source of the diversification benefit
Systematic (market) risk: risk that affects all firms, like recessions, interest rate shocks, and oil price changes; cannot be diversified away because it hits every stock

Idiosyncratic (firm-specific) risk: risk unique to a firm, like management changes, product failures, or lawsuits; can be diversified away by holding many uncorrelated stocks, so markets do not compensate investors for bearing it

The efficient frontier: the set of portfolios offering the highest expected return for each level of risk, found by minimizing variance subject to a return target; rational investors should only hold portfolios on this frontier
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compute two-asset portfolio statistics live. Stock A has expected return 12% and standard deviation 20%; Stock B has expected return 8% and standard deviation 15%; correlation is 0.3. Equal-weight portfolio (50/50): E(Rp) = 0.5 x 12% + 0.5 x 8% = 10%. Portfolio variance = 0.25 x 0.04 + 0.25 x 0.0225 + 2 x 0.5 x 0.5 x 0.3 x 0.20 x 0.15 = 0.01 + 0.005625 + 0.0045 = 0.020125. Standard deviation = sqrt(0.020125) = 14.19%. Compare: Stock A alone is 20% SD, Stock B alone is 15% SD, the equal-weight portfolio is only 14.19% SD, lower than either asset individually. Now repeat with correlation = -0.5: portfolio variance = 0.01 + 0.005625 - 0.0075 = 0.008125, SD = 9.01%. Dramatically lower. Discuss: negative correlation is the diversifier's dream because the two assets hedge each other. Show the shape of the efficient frontier in (return, SD) space as a Markowitz bullet.
setup_requirements: Excel spreadsheet with returns data for 10 stocks across 5 sectors over 5 years
Covariance matrix calculation template with =COVARIANCE and =CORREL functions
Efficient frontier construction worksheet using Excel Solver
Historical return data from Yahoo Finance or CRSP

review.value: Because idiosyncratic risk is uncompensated, diversifying it away does not cost expected return; an investor holding a single stock is bearing risk for which the market will not pay them, a strictly dominated position

review.key: Correlation Matters More Than Individual Volatility
review.value: Two very volatile stocks with correlation -0.9 combine into a portfolio with very low volatility; two modestly volatile stocks with correlation +0.95 combine into a portfolio not much less volatile than its components; correlation is the key ingredient

Empirical studies (Evans and Archer 1968, Statman 1987) show that a random portfolio of about 20-30 stocks captures most of the diversification benefit; beyond 50 stocks, the marginal reduction in idiosyncratic risk is negligible relative to transaction costs

review.key: Systematic Risk Is All That Matters for Pricing
review.value: In a world of well-diversified investors, only systematic (market) risk is priced; this is the foundation of CAPM and explains why a stock's beta, not its total volatility, determines its required return

class_schedule.topic: The Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) and Beta
learning_objectives: State the CAPM equation and interpret each component: risk-free rate, beta, and equity risk premium
Estimate beta from historical returns using regression and interpret the intercept (alpha) and R-squared
Compute required returns for individual stocks and apply them to valuation and capital budgeting
Understand the theoretical foundations, empirical tests, and limitations of CAPM, including the Fama-French extensions
readings: Brealey, Myers & Allen, Principles of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 8-9 - Portfolio Theory and CAPM, Risk and the Cost of Capital
Ross, Westerfield & Jordan, Fundamentals of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 13 - Return, Risk, and the Security Market Line
Berk & DeMarzo, Corporate Finance (5th ed.), Ch. 12 - Estimating the Cost of Capital
Fama & French, 'The Cross-Section of Expected Stock Returns' (1992), Journal of Finance
Sharpe, 'Capital Asset Prices: A Theory of Market Equilibrium under Conditions of Risk' (1964)

lecture_notes.introduction: The Capital Asset Pricing Model, developed independently by William Sharpe, John Lintner, and Jan Mossin in the 1960s, answers the question left hanging at the end of the last session: if only systematic risk is priced, how much return does the market require for bearing it? The answer is the famous CAPM equation: E(R_i) = R_f + beta_i x (E(R_m) - R_f), which says that the expected return on any asset equals the risk-free rate plus the asset's beta times the market risk premium. Beta is the quantity of systematic risk (sensitivity to market movements), and the equity risk premium is the price of systematic risk (compensation per unit of market risk borne). CAPM is the most widely used, most widely criticized, and most widely reapplied model in finance: every CFO who computes cost of equity, every equity analyst who estimates required return, and every investor who benchmarks performance relies on CAPM or a close cousin of it. Empirically, CAPM does not work perfectly; Fama and French (1992) showed that size and value factors explain cross-sectional returns better than beta alone, launching the multi-factor model literature. But CAPM remains the workhorse because it is simple, intuitive, and approximately right. In this session we derive CAPM from portfolio theory, estimate beta for real companies using regression, apply CAPM to compute the required return for Tesla, Apple, and Walmart, and honestly discuss the model's limitations so you know when to trust it and when to supplement it with extensions.
key_concepts: CAPM equation: E(R_i) = R_f + beta_i x (E(R_m) - R_f); the security market line (SML) in return-beta space has intercept R_f and slope equal to the equity risk premium
Risk-free rate R_f: the Treasury yield matching the valuation horizon; the 10-year Treasury for long-term equity valuation, short-term T-bill for short-horizon applications

Beta: measures sensitivity to market returns; beta = Cov(R_i, R_m) / Var(R_m); also the slope coefficient from regressing R_i on R_m, so beta can be estimated empirically with a few years of monthly returns
Market risk premium (E(R_m) - R_f): the expected excess return of the market over the risk-free rate; historically about 5-6% in the U.S., though forward-looking estimates tend to be lower

Regression of stock returns on market returns: R_i = alpha + beta x R_m + epsilon; the alpha is the abnormal return not explained by market exposure, R-squared is the fraction of variance attributable to market movements

Leverage and beta: equity beta rises with financial leverage; unlevered (asset) beta can be derived as beta_equity / (1 + (1-T)(D/E)), used in comparable firm analysis to separate operating risk from financing risk
Applications of CAPM: cost of equity for WACC, required return for DDM and DCF valuation, benchmark return for performance evaluation (Jensen's alpha), hurdle rate for project NPV

Fama-French three-factor model: adds size (SMB, small minus big) and value (HML, high minus low book-to-market) factors to explain returns that CAPM misses; evidence that small-cap and value stocks earn higher returns than CAPM predicts
Carhart four-factor extension: adds momentum (WML, winners minus losers); five- and six-factor extensions add profitability and investment factors

Behavioral critiques: bubbles, crashes, and persistent anomalies suggest markets are not perfectly efficient; CAPM is a useful approximation but should be combined with judgment, not applied mechanically
lecture_notes.demonstration: Estimate Apple's beta and cost of equity. Pull 60 months of Apple and S&P 500 returns from Yahoo Finance. Run Excel regression: =SLOPE(Apple_returns, SP500_returns) returns roughly 1.20 (typical for Apple in recent years). With R_f = 4.5% (current 10-year Treasury) and equity risk premium = 5.5%, CAPM gives required return = 4.5% + 1.20 x 5.5% = 4.5% + 6.6% = 11.1%. Compare to Walmart (beta around 0.55): required return = 4.5% + 0.55 x 5.5% = 7.5%. Compare to Tesla (beta around 2.0): required return = 4.5% + 2.0 x 5.5% = 15.5%. Interpret: Tesla's high beta means investors require a much higher expected return to hold it, consistent with its wild price swings; Walmart's low beta means its expected return is only modestly above the risk-free rate because it behaves like a defensive stock. Apply each cost of equity to a DDM valuation and show how different results emerge.
setup_requirements: Excel with Analysis ToolPak enabled for regression and SLOPE function
5 years of monthly return data for 15 stocks and the S&P 500 index
Current Treasury yields and Damodaran's equity risk premium estimates from his NYU web page
Beta reference values from Bloomberg, Yahoo Finance, or Morningstar for comparison

review.value: Beta > 1 means the stock is more volatile than the market in direction and magnitude; beta < 1 means less volatile; beta = 1 means it moves with the market on average; a beta of 0 means the stock is uncorrelated with the market (rare in practice)

The equity risk premium is the single most debated input in finance: historical realized returns suggest 6-7%, implied/forward-looking estimates suggest 4-5%, and the choice significantly changes cost of equity for high-beta stocks

A company's equity beta reflects both operating risk and financial leverage; when comparing firms with different capital structures, unlever the betas to isolate operating risk and then relever at the target structure, a crucial step in M&A and LBO analysis

Empirical failures (size, value, momentum, profitability) motivate multi-factor models; Fama-French is the standard upgrade, and practitioners often supplement CAPM with an industry risk premium or country risk adjustment for emerging markets, following Damodaran's approach

learning_objectives: Compute the weighted average cost of capital (WACC) using market values of debt and equity and the after-tax cost of debt
Estimate each component of WACC: cost of equity (CAPM), cost of debt (yield to maturity), and appropriate weights
Apply the correct WACC to project evaluation based on risk and capital structure considerations
Understand how WACC changes with leverage, tax rates, and business risk, and why it is the single most important number in corporate finance
readings: Brealey, Myers & Allen, Principles of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 9 - Risk and the Cost of Capital
Ross, Westerfield & Jordan, Fundamentals of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 14 - Cost of Capital
Berk & DeMarzo, Corporate Finance (5th ed.), Ch. 12-18 - Cost of Capital and Capital Structure
Damodaran, Corporate Finance: Theory and Practice (2nd ed.), Ch. 4 - The Cost of Capital

lecture_notes.introduction: Every company that uses a mix of debt and equity to finance its operations has a cost of capital, and that cost is the hurdle rate any investment must clear to create value. If the WACC is 9% and a project earns 8%, the project destroys value; if it earns 10%, it creates value. This is why companies that consistently invest at returns above their cost of capital, think Apple, Visa, and Microsoft, compound shareholder wealth at spectacular rates, while companies that invest below their cost of capital (GE Power in the 2010s, the airline industry in most decades) destroy it. WACC is the weighted average of the after-tax cost of debt and the cost of equity, weighted by the proportion of each in the capital structure using market values, not book values. The logic is simple: the firm uses a blend of capital sources, and any project the firm funds must earn enough to satisfy both debt holders and equity holders. The math is also simple: WACC = (E/V) x Re + (D/V) x Rd x (1-T), where E is market value of equity, D is market value of debt, V = E + D, Re is the cost of equity from CAPM, Rd is the yield to maturity on debt, and T is the marginal tax rate. But the devil is in the inputs: which interest rate, which beta, which risk premium, which weights, and for which project. An industrial conglomerate like pre-breakup GE had businesses with wildly different risk profiles, and applying a single corporate WACC to all of them led to chronic misallocation. A division-level WACC, built from comparable pure-play firms, is the correct approach, and one of the most important analytical skills in corporate finance.
key_concepts: WACC formula: WACC = (E/V) x R_e + (D/V) x R_d x (1 - T_c); the tax shield on interest makes after-tax cost of debt lower than pretax
Cost of equity from CAPM: R_e = R_f + beta x (R_m - R_f); for firms with thin trading or limited history, use industry averages or unlevered betas from comparables
Cost of debt: use current yield to maturity on the firm's outstanding bonds, not the coupon rate or historical borrowing rate; if the firm has multiple bond issues, use a weighted average YTM
After-tax cost of debt: R_d x (1 - T_c) captures the tax shield since interest is deductible; the effective cost of debt is 8% pretax becomes 6% after-tax at a 25% corporate tax rate
Market value weights: E and D should be market values, because WACC is a forward-looking opportunity cost; book values reflect historical costs and distort current capital structure mix

Target vs current capital structure: firms typically have a target D/E ratio; use the target (not current) when evaluating projects that will be financed in the long run, because short-term deviations wash out

Division-level WACC: conglomerates should compute a separate WACC for each business unit using comparable firms in that unit's industry; applying corporate WACC to all projects systematically over-invests in high-risk units and under-invests in low-risk units

Cost of capital vs required return: WACC is the required return on the firm's overall capital; project hurdle rate is the required return on a specific project's risk; these differ when project risk differs from average firm risk
International cost of capital: add country risk premium (Damodaran's approach) or use local risk-free rate and market risk premium for projects in emerging markets
Why WACC matters: a 1% error in WACC on a 30-year DCF valuation produces errors of 15-25% in the resulting price, making input precision essential
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compute WACC for a company live. A firm has $600 million market value of equity and $400 million market value of debt (so V = $1,000M, E/V = 60%, D/V = 40%). Beta is 1.1, risk-free rate is 4.5%, equity risk premium is 5.5%. Cost of equity = 4.5% + 1.1 x 5.5% = 10.55%. The firm's outstanding bonds trade at a YTM of 6.2%. Corporate tax rate is 25%. After-tax cost of debt = 6.2% x (1 - 0.25) = 4.65%. WACC = 0.60 x 10.55% + 0.40 x 4.65% = 6.33% + 1.86% = 8.19%. Then show how WACC changes: if the firm doubles its debt to $800M while equity falls to $400M (total $1.2B), the new weights are E/V = 33.3%, D/V = 66.7%; equity beta rises with leverage to around 1.65, new cost of equity = 4.5% + 1.65 x 5.5% = 13.58%; interest rate rises as credit risk increases, new cost of debt = 7.5% pretax, after-tax = 5.63%. New WACC = 0.333 x 13.58% + 0.667 x 5.63% = 4.52% + 3.75% = 8.27%. A small change, illustrating the Modigliani-Miller intuition we will develop in week 12: WACC is relatively flat across capital structures in the absence of distress costs.
setup_requirements: Current 10-K filings for 5 firms with varying capital structures (Apple, Ford, Walmart, AT&T, a regulated utility)
Excel WACC template with cost of equity, cost of debt, weights, and tax rate inputs
Bloomberg or market data for bond yields and beta estimates
Damodaran's industry cost of capital database from his NYU web page

review.value: WACC uses market values because those are the amounts actual investors have at risk today; book equity reflects historical accounting, and using book weights can bias WACC significantly, especially for firms with long histories or large write-offs

Interest is tax-deductible, so $1 of interest reduces taxes by T_c dollars; the after-tax cost of debt captures this, and is the reason leverage reduces WACC (up to a point), which drives much of capital structure theory

One WACC fits one risk profile; a diversified firm needs multiple WACCs, computed from pure-play comparables, or it will consistently misallocate capital toward riskier divisions (where the corporate WACC is too low) and away from safer divisions (where it is too high)

In a 30-year DCF, a WACC change from 9% to 8% can raise the computed valuation by 20% or more; this is both a warning (be careful with inputs) and an opportunity (the difference between good and bad investments often comes down to correctly pricing risk)

class_schedule.topic: Capital Budgeting I: NPV, IRR, Payback, Profitability Index
learning_objectives: Compute net present value (NPV), internal rate of return (IRR), payback period, discounted payback, and profitability index for a project
Explain why NPV is the theoretically correct decision rule and identify situations where IRR and other criteria can mislead
Handle special cases: mutually exclusive projects, capital rationing, unequal project lives, and non-conventional cash flows
Apply capital budgeting tools to real project decisions including replacement analysis, expansion projects, and new product launches
readings: Brealey, Myers & Allen, Principles of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 5-6 - Why NPV Leads to Better Investment Decisions, Making Investment Decisions
Ross, Westerfield & Jordan, Fundamentals of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 9-10 - Net Present Value and Other Investment Criteria, Making Capital Investment Decisions
Berk & DeMarzo, Corporate Finance (5th ed.), Ch. 7-8 - Investment Decision Rules, Fundamentals of Capital Budgeting
Damodaran, Corporate Finance: Theory and Practice (2nd ed.), Ch. 5-6 - Investment Decision Rules

lecture_notes.introduction: Capital budgeting is the process of deciding which long-term investments a firm should make, and it is the most important application of everything we have learned so far. A firm that consistently picks good projects, where 'good' means projects that create more value than they cost, grows and prospers; a firm that consistently picks bad projects goes bankrupt, no matter how skilled its marketing or operations. The net present value rule, NPV = sum of discounted expected cash flows minus initial investment, accept if NPV > 0, is the theoretically correct answer to the capital budgeting question. It is correct because it directly measures the dollar value added to shareholders: a $5 million NPV project creates $5 million of value that can be distributed to or reinvested by owners. Every other criterion, IRR, payback, profitability index, is either equivalent to NPV in simple cases or actively misleading in complex cases, and understanding when each criterion works is essential because you will encounter them all in practice. When Amazon built its first AWS data centers in the mid-2000s, the initial NPV calculation had to account for enormous capital costs, uncertain cloud demand, and long payback horizons; the project looked unattractive by simple payback rules but created one of the most valuable businesses in history. When Tesla decided to build its Gigafactories, the decision was driven by NPV-style analysis comparing future battery cost savings and production capacity against billions in upfront capital. Conversely, when airlines historically invested in fleet expansion during boom cycles based on IRR calculations that ignored the eventual bust, they systematically destroyed value. This session gives you the tools to make the right investment decisions, and to recognize when someone is using the wrong ones.
key_concepts: Net present value (NPV): NPV = sum from t=0 to n of CF_t / (1 + r)^t, where CF_0 is typically negative (initial investment) and CF_1 through CF_n are expected future cash flows; accept the project if NPV > 0

Internal rate of return (IRR): the discount rate that makes NPV = 0; accept if IRR > cost of capital; equivalent to NPV for standalone conventional projects but can mislead for mutually exclusive or non-conventional projects

Payback period: the number of years required to recover the initial investment; simple but ignores time value of money and cash flows beyond the payback year, so it is a liquidity rule, not a value rule
Discounted payback: same as payback but using discounted cash flows; improves on simple payback but still ignores post-payback cash flows
Profitability index (PI): PI = PV of future cash flows / initial investment; useful under capital rationing because it measures NPV per dollar of investment

Mutually exclusive projects: you can only choose one; NPV is the correct criterion, not IRR, because IRR can favor smaller projects with higher percent returns over larger projects with higher absolute value creation

Non-conventional cash flows (multiple sign changes): projects with alternating positive and negative cash flows can have multiple IRRs or no real IRR at all; NPV gives a unique answer while IRR becomes ambiguous
Unequal project lives: when comparing a 5-year project to a 10-year project, compute the equivalent annual annuity (EAA) or replicate the shorter project to align horizons

Crossover rate: the discount rate at which two projects have equal NPV; above the crossover, the lower-IRR but higher-initial-investment project may dominate, and IRR ranking inverts the correct NPV ranking

Reinvestment rate assumption: IRR implicitly assumes interim cash flows can be reinvested at IRR, which is often unrealistic; NPV assumes reinvestment at the cost of capital, which is the correct opportunity cost
lecture_notes.demonstration: Evaluate a project with $100,000 initial investment and cash flows of $30,000 in year 1, $40,000 in year 2, $35,000 in year 3, $30,000 in year 4, and $25,000 in year 5, at a 10% discount rate. NPV = -100,000 + 30,000/1.10 + 40,000/1.21 + 35,000/1.331 + 30,000/1.4641 + 25,000/1.6105. Compute each: -100,000 + 27,273 + 33,058 + 26,296 + 20,491 + 15,523 = 22,641. NPV = $22,641 > 0, so accept. IRR is found by setting NPV = 0: using Excel =IRR function on [-100000, 30000, 40000, 35000, 30000, 25000] returns IRR = 18.6%. Since 18.6% > 10% cost of capital, accept. Payback period: cumulative cash flows are 30, 70, 105, 135, 160; cross 100 during year 3, so payback = 2 + 30/35 = 2.86 years. Discounted payback uses discounted CFs: 27.27, 33.06, 26.30, 20.49, 15.52; cumulative 27.27, 60.33, 86.63, 107.12; cross 100 during year 4, so discounted payback = 3 + 13.37/20.49 = 3.65 years. Profitability index = (27.27 + 33.06 + 26.30 + 20.49 + 15.52) / 100 = 122.64 / 100 = 1.226. All four criteria agree: accept. Then show a case where they disagree by introducing a mutually exclusive alternative and discuss which to trust.
setup_requirements: Excel with =NPV, =IRR, =XNPV, =XIRR, =MIRR functions
Financial calculator with CF (cash flow) register and NPV/IRR computation
Sample project evaluation worksheets with conventional and non-conventional cash flows
Real project case study: a chain coffee retailer evaluating a new store or a manufacturer evaluating an equipment purchase

review.value: NPV directly measures dollar value added and uses the cost of capital as the opportunity cost; every other rule is an approximation or simplification that can give wrong answers in complex cases, so when criteria disagree, trust NPV

For mutually exclusive projects, IRR can rank a small high-percentage project above a larger modest-percentage project even though the larger project creates more value; for non-conventional cash flows, multiple IRRs make the rule ambiguous; always supplement IRR with NPV for these cases

Payback measures how fast you get your money back, which matters for risk-averse or liquidity-constrained firms, but it ignores the time value of money and all post-payback cash flows, so it should never be the sole decision criterion

When capital is limited and projects are divisible, maximize the total NPV per dollar invested by ranking projects on PI and accepting in order; when projects are not divisible, use integer programming or exhaustive enumeration for the optimal combination

class_schedule.topic: Capital Budgeting II: Cash Flow Estimation and Sensitivity Analysis
learning_objectives: Estimate incremental after-tax cash flows for a capital project including revenues, expenses, depreciation tax shield, working capital, and terminal value
Distinguish between accounting income and cash flow and correctly handle sunk costs, opportunity costs, side effects, and allocated overheads
Apply sensitivity analysis, scenario analysis, and break-even analysis to understand the risk profile of a project
Recognize the most common cash flow estimation errors in practice and develop a disciplined framework for avoiding them
readings: Brealey, Myers & Allen, Principles of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 6 - Making Investment Decisions with the NPV Rule
Ross, Westerfield & Jordan, Fundamentals of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 10 - Making Capital Investment Decisions
Berk & DeMarzo, Corporate Finance (5th ed.), Ch. 8 - Fundamentals of Capital Budgeting
Damodaran, Corporate Finance: Theory and Practice (2nd ed.), Ch. 6 - Estimating Cash Flows on Projects

lecture_notes.introduction: Good NPV calculations depend on good cash flow estimates, and cash flow estimation is where most real capital budgeting mistakes happen. The formulas are easy; the judgment is hard. In practice, a manager proposing a project has strong incentives to inflate expected revenues, understate costs, and exclude inconvenient cash flows; the finance team's job is to apply a disciplined framework that extracts the right numbers regardless of political pressures. The central principle is that only incremental, after-tax, cash flows matter: incremental means the difference between accepting and rejecting the project, after-tax means after the corporate tax bill is paid, and cash means actual dollar inflows and outflows, not accounting accruals. This principle is violated constantly. Sunk costs (money already spent) are often included even though they are irrelevant. Opportunity costs (the value of using a resource you already own) are often omitted. Side effects on other projects (cannibalization) are conveniently forgotten. Allocated overheads unrelated to the project are included to satisfy internal accounting conventions. When Disney evaluated the Fox acquisition, the incremental cash flow analysis had to distinguish the revenues Fox assets would generate for Disney above what Disney would earn without them, and the synergies had to be carefully estimated so as not to double-count. In this session we build a rigorous cash flow template, work through a realistic project example with all the pieces, apply sensitivity analysis to understand which inputs matter most, and introduce scenario analysis and break-even analysis as tools for assessing project risk before committing capital.
key_concepts: Incremental cash flow = cash flow with project minus cash flow without project; only cash flows that change as a result of the decision are relevant

Sunk costs: expenses already incurred that cannot be recovered (e.g., feasibility study, market research); always excluded from incremental cash flow analysis because they happen whether or not the project is accepted

Opportunity costs: the value of using a resource the firm already owns that could otherwise be sold or deployed elsewhere; must be included (e.g., using a building the firm owns has an opportunity cost equal to the rental income foregone)
Side effects: cannibalization of existing product sales, complementary effects that boost other product sales, and erosion of brand equity; must be included at the incremental net effect

Net working capital changes: projects typically require investment in receivables and inventory and generate some payables; NWC increases are cash outflows, decreases are cash inflows, and NWC is typically recovered at project end

Depreciation tax shield: depreciation is not a cash flow, but it reduces taxable income, so the after-tax cash flow is (Revenue - Cost) x (1-T) + Depreciation x T; the tax shield equals Depreciation x T
Terminal value: the cash value of the project at end of life, including salvage value (net of tax on gains or losses vs book value) and recovery of net working capital

Sensitivity analysis: vary one input at a time (revenue, cost, discount rate) to see how NPV responds; identifies which assumptions have the largest impact on project value and where to focus due diligence

Scenario analysis: specify a small number of coherent scenarios (base, upside, downside, stress) with internally consistent input combinations; provides a richer risk picture than one-at-a-time sensitivity
Break-even analysis: find the value of a key input at which NPV = 0 (e.g., sales volume, price); if break-even is far from expected, the project has a safety margin; if close, the project is risky
lecture_notes.demonstration: Estimate cash flows for a new product launch. Initial investment: $500,000 in equipment (5-year straight-line depreciation to zero) and $100,000 in working capital. Expected annual revenues: $400,000; annual cash costs: $200,000; tax rate 25%. Compute annual after-tax operating cash flow: (400,000 - 200,000) x (1 - 0.25) + 100,000 x 0.25 = 150,000 + 25,000 = $175,000. At end of year 5, equipment is sold for $50,000 (taxable gain of $50,000 x 0.25 = $12,500 tax), net salvage = $37,500, and working capital of $100,000 is recovered. Year 5 total cash flow = 175,000 + 37,500 + 100,000 = $312,500. Full cash flow stream: Year 0 = -600,000, Years 1-4 = 175,000, Year 5 = 312,500. At 10% discount rate, NPV = -600,000 + 175,000 x [(1 - 1.10^-4)/0.10] + 312,500/1.10^5 = -600,000 + 175,000 x 3.1699 + 312,500/1.6105 = -600,000 + 554,733 + 194,043 = 148,776. NPV = $148,776, accept. Then sensitivity: if revenue is 10% lower, new after-tax operating CF = (360,000 - 200,000) x 0.75 + 25,000 = 120,000 + 25,000 = 145,000; recompute NPV. This single change drops NPV dramatically, showing revenue is the key driver and should be scrutinized.
setup_requirements: Excel cash flow template with revenue, cost, depreciation, tax, NWC, and terminal value rows
Tornado chart add-in or manual construction for sensitivity visualization
Scenario manager tool in Excel Data menu for multi-scenario analysis
Sample project case: new manufacturing line, new store opening, or new product launch

review.value: Once money is spent and not recoverable, it is gone whether or not you proceed with the project; including sunk costs in NPV biases decisions toward continuing projects that should be killed, a classic failure documented in behavioral finance (the sunk cost fallacy)

Depreciation is an accounting expense that is not a cash outflow; add it back after the tax shield. Net income is not cash flow, and using net income in NPV systematically understates project value, especially for capital-intensive projects

A growing project needs growing NWC, and this is a real cash outflow each year that is often forgotten; forgetting it creates the illusion that projects generate more cash than they actually do and leads to overinvestment

A project can have positive expected NPV but be extremely risky if its value depends on an uncertain input; sensitivity and scenario analysis identify which assumptions need the most due diligence and which scenarios the firm can survive

class_schedule.topic: Capital Structure I: Modigliani-Miller and Trade-off Theory
learning_objectives: State the Modigliani-Miller Propositions I and II with and without taxes and explain their intuitive meaning
Quantify the tax shield benefit of debt and its contribution to firm value
Identify the costs of financial distress (direct and indirect) and their role in the trade-off theory of capital structure
Apply trade-off theory to real firms and explain observed capital structure differences across industries
readings: Brealey, Myers & Allen, Principles of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 17-18 - Does Debt Policy Matter?, How Much Should a Corporation Borrow?
Ross, Westerfield & Jordan, Fundamentals of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 16 - Financial Leverage and Capital Structure Policy
Berk & DeMarzo, Corporate Finance (5th ed.), Ch. 14-16 - Capital Structure in Perfect Markets, Taxes, and Distress
Modigliani & Miller, 'The Cost of Capital, Corporation Finance and the Theory of Investment' (1958), American Economic Review

lecture_notes.introduction: In 1958, Franco Modigliani and Merton Miller published a paper that overturned conventional thinking about how firms should finance themselves and eventually earned both authors Nobel Prizes. Their first proposition (MM I), under the assumptions of perfect markets, no taxes, no bankruptcy costs, no information asymmetry, and identical borrowing rates for firms and individuals, states that the value of the firm is independent of its capital structure. In plain English, the size of the pie does not depend on how it is sliced between debt and equity holders. This result is counterintuitive and initially struck practitioners as wrong, because they believed leverage creates value by giving shareholders higher returns. MM showed that the higher returns to equity come with higher risk exactly offsetting the benefit, leaving firm value unchanged. MM II then shows explicitly that the cost of equity rises linearly with the debt-to-equity ratio: R_e = R_a + (R_a - R_d) x (D/E), where R_a is the asset (unlevered) return. The striking implication of MM I is that, in a world without taxes or distress, capital structure is irrelevant, and therefore any observed effect of leverage on value must come from violating one of the MM assumptions. Taxes are the most important violation: because interest is deductible, levered firms pay less tax and leave more value for claim holders, so the value of the levered firm equals the value of the unlevered firm plus the present value of the tax shield. This would imply firms should be 100% debt-financed, which is obviously wrong, because at very high leverage the probability and cost of financial distress become significant and offset the tax shield. The resulting trade-off theory balances the tax shield against distress costs and predicts an optimal interior capital structure.
