Observed industry patterns, utilities and real estate at high leverage, technology and biotech at low leverage, line up with what the theory predicts.
key_concepts: MM Proposition I (no taxes): V_L = V_U; the value of a levered firm equals the value of an unlevered firm; capital structure does not affect firm value in perfect markets
MM Proposition II (no taxes): R_e = R_a + (R_a - R_d) x (D/E); the cost of equity rises linearly with leverage, because equity holders bear more risk as debt holders' senior claim grows

MM intuition: a firm's cash flows depend on its assets, not on who claims them; shuffling the claims between debt and equity holders does not change the total cash flow generated, so total value is unchanged

Homemade leverage: investors can undo or replicate any capital structure by borrowing or lending on their own account, so firms gain nothing by leverage arbitrage; this argument depends on investors being able to borrow at the same rate as firms, a strong assumption
MM with corporate taxes: V_L = V_U + T_c x D; the tax shield on debt adds value equal to the tax rate times debt outstanding, pushing optimal leverage toward 100%
Direct costs of financial distress: legal fees, advisor fees, court costs, and administrative expenses of bankruptcy; typically 3-20% of firm value depending on the complexity of the restructuring

Indirect costs of financial distress: lost customers (who fear warranty support will disappear), reluctant suppliers (who demand cash up front), employee defections, diversion of management attention, and sub-optimal operating decisions forced by liquidity constraints
Trade-off theory: V_L = V_U + PV(tax shield) - PV(distress costs); optimal leverage is where the marginal tax shield benefit equals the marginal distress cost

Industry patterns predicted by trade-off theory: stable cash-generative industries (utilities, REITs, telecom) can sustain high leverage; volatile, growth, or intangible-asset industries (biotech, software, semiconductors) rationally use little debt

Agency costs of debt and equity: debt creates risk-shifting and underinvestment incentives (Myers 1977), while free cash flow equity creates over-investment incentives (Jensen 1986); these agency considerations refine trade-off theory
lecture_notes.demonstration: Work through a numerical MM example. Unlevered firm generates $100 million EBIT forever; unlevered cost of capital is 10%; corporate tax rate 25%. Unlevered firm value: V_U = 100 x (1 - 0.25) / 0.10 = 75/0.10 = $750 million. Now the firm issues $300 million of debt to repurchase equity (so D = $300M). Tax shield value = T_c x D = 0.25 x 300 = $75 million. Levered firm value V_L = 750 + 75 = $825 million. The firm created $75 million of value by issuing debt. Now introduce distress costs: assume the expected PV of distress costs at this leverage level is $20 million. Adjusted V_L = 750 + 75 - 20 = $805 million. If instead the firm levered up to $500 million debt, the tax shield grows to 0.25 x 500 = $125 million, but distress costs might grow more than proportionally to, say, $80 million. V_L = 750 + 125 - 80 = $795 million, lower than the $805M at $300M debt. The optimum is somewhere in between, found by balancing marginal benefits and costs.
setup_requirements: MM Proposition cheat sheet with and without taxes
Industry capital structure data from Damodaran's NYU database (debt ratios by industry)
Excel MM simulation template showing V_L for various D levels with and without distress costs
Historical case studies: LBO firms (Carlyle, Blackstone), utility holding companies, and biotech startups

review.key: MM Is A Baseline, Not A Prescription
review.value: MM's irrelevance result assumes away the very frictions (taxes, distress costs, information asymmetry) that make capital structure matter in practice; the value of MM is that it isolates exactly which frictions drive leverage decisions, so we know where to look for the benefit

review.key: Tax Shield Is Real But Bounded
review.value: Interest deductibility is a direct, measurable benefit of debt; but it is capped by earnings (no tax bill to shield if the firm has losses), and the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017 in the U.S. added limits on interest deductibility that reduced this benefit for highly levered firms

review.key: Distress Costs Are Larger Than Most Think
review.value: Academic estimates put direct costs at 3-5% of pre-distress firm value, but indirect costs (lost customers, reluctant suppliers, management distraction) can push total costs to 20% or more; this is why firms stop leveraging up well before 100% debt, even though the tax shield keeps accumulating

The trade-off theory predicts that industries with stable cash flows and tangible collateral (utilities, airlines during good times, real estate) should and do use more debt, while industries with volatile cash flows and intangible assets (software, biotech) should and do use less; the cross-sectional pattern is strong evidence for trade-off theory

class_schedule.topic: Capital Structure II: Pecking Order and Optimal Leverage in Practice
learning_objectives: State the pecking order theory of financing and explain its information-asymmetry foundation
Compare the predictions of trade-off theory and pecking order theory against empirical evidence
Evaluate how firms choose between retained earnings, debt, hybrid securities, and new equity issuances in practice
Apply capital structure reasoning to real corporate decisions including IPOs, seasoned offerings, debt issuances, and leveraged buyouts
readings: Brealey, Myers & Allen, Principles of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 18 - How Much Should a Corporation Borrow
Myers & Majluf, 'Corporate Financing and Investment Decisions When Firms Have Information That Investors Do Not Have' (1984)
Berk & DeMarzo, Corporate Finance (5th ed.), Ch. 16 - Financial Distress, Managerial Incentives, and Information
Ross, Westerfield, Jaffe & Jordan, Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 17 - Capital Structure: Limits to the Use of Debt

lecture_notes.introduction: Trade-off theory is elegant but incomplete. It predicts that firms should maintain a stable target leverage ratio set by balancing tax shields against distress costs. But empirical reality looks different: profitable firms tend to use less debt, contrary to trade-off prediction (more profit means more taxes to shield, so more debt); firms raise capital in a clear hierarchy, preferring internal funds first, then debt, then equity; and equity issuances are rare and associated with negative stock price reactions. Myers and Majluf (1984) explained this pattern with the pecking order theory: because managers know more about the firm's true value than outside investors do, any decision to issue equity signals that managers think the stock is overvalued, which causes the price to drop when an issuance is announced. Knowing this, managers avoid equity issuance except when absolutely necessary, and use internal funds first (no signaling cost), debt second (mild signaling cost because interest obligations are a form of commitment), and equity last. The pecking order explains several puzzles: why the most profitable firms have the least debt (they generate enough internal cash flow to avoid needing external financing at all), why equity issuances are concentrated in high-valuation market periods (Myers and Majluf's signal is weakened when the market is already optimistic), and why debt issuances are far more common than equity issuances in normal times. Tesla's capital-raising history is an instructive counter-example and exception: Tesla repeatedly issued equity through its growth phase, taking advantage of high valuations during bull markets (2020 in particular) to fund operations and expansion. This worked because Tesla's stock was often overvalued relative to fundamentals and the signal was overwhelmed by bullish sentiment. In this session we compare the two theories, work through Tesla and Microsoft as detailed cases, and introduce the practical considerations that actually drive CFO decisions: financial flexibility, credit ratings, debt covenants, and the market timing that traditional theory ignores.
key_concepts: Pecking order: internal funds first, then debt, then equity; driven by information asymmetry between managers and outside investors, not by a target debt ratio

Myers-Majluf signaling: equity issuances signal overvaluation because managers know more than investors; the average stock price reaction to a seasoned equity offering announcement is approximately -3% to -4%
Internal funds advantage: retained earnings have no signaling cost and no flotation cost, so they are the cheapest source of capital; profitable firms consequently use less external financing

Debt advantage over equity: debt has a fixed interest rate regardless of firm type, so it is less information-sensitive than equity, which is why Myers-Majluf firms prefer debt over equity when external funds are needed

Financial flexibility: firms maintain unused debt capacity to fund unexpected investment opportunities or weather downturns; this explains why many profitable firms (Apple, Alphabet, Microsoft pre-2020) have kept low leverage despite large tax shield benefits

Credit rating considerations: firms target specific credit ratings (e.g., single A or better) to maintain market access and low interest rates; ratings-driven capital structure is common among investment-grade firms

Debt covenants: bond indentures and bank loan agreements specify maximum leverage, minimum interest coverage, dividend restrictions, and other constraints; covenant-driven deleveraging is a major factor in distressed firms

Market timing: firms issue equity when valuations are high and repurchase when valuations are low; Baker and Wurgler (2002) show that current capital structure reflects the cumulative history of market timing decisions

Leveraged buyouts (LBOs): private equity firms use high leverage (typically 60-80% debt) to acquire target firms, amplifying returns on equity and exploiting the tax shield; the KKR/RJR Nabisco deal (1988, $25 billion, documented in 'Barbarians at the Gate') is the archetype

Payout policy interaction: firms that return cash through dividends and buybacks (Apple, Microsoft, Johnson & Johnson) do so in part to reduce free cash flow agency problems (Jensen 1986) and to keep leverage near target levels
lecture_notes.demonstration: Trace Microsoft's capital structure history. Through the 1990s, Microsoft had essentially no debt, large cash balances, and paid no dividends, consistent with a growing high-margin software firm with internal cash flow exceeding investment needs. In 2003, Microsoft initiated its first regular dividend and a special $3 per share dividend that returned $32 billion to shareholders, signaling the shift from growth to maturity. Since then, Microsoft has combined modest debt (for credit rating and tax shield purposes), growing dividends, and large buyback programs. By contrast, Tesla issued equity repeatedly during its growth phase: a major 2020 secondary offering of roughly $5 billion at $767 per share, another $5 billion in December 2020 at high prices, and continued equity issuance as stock appreciated, because its internal cash flow was insufficient to fund the Gigafactories and because its stock was often trading at valuations its managers considered rich. Contrast both with the KKR/RJR Nabisco LBO, where KKR took a $25 billion company and financed the acquisition with roughly 90% debt, betting that cost cuts and asset sales would service the debt while equity returns would be amplified by leverage. Compute the return to KKR equity under different operating outcomes to show the leverage amplification.
setup_requirements: Tesla historical 10-Ks and secondary offering prospectuses from SEC EDGAR
Microsoft historical 10-Ks showing debt, cash, dividend, and buyback history
Bryan Burrough and John Helyar, 'Barbarians at the Gate' (1990), excerpts for LBO case
Capital IQ data on current industry-median debt ratios for tech, utility, and industrial sectors

review.value: Trade-off theory predicts the opposite (more profits means more taxes to shield), but pecking order correctly predicts it: profitable firms generate enough internal cash flow to avoid external financing, so their observed leverage reflects historical investment funding rather than a target

review.key: Equity Issuance Is A Negative Signal
review.value: Average stock price reactions to equity offerings are -3% to -4%, consistent with Myers-Majluf, which is why firms avoid issuing equity unless they have to, and tend to issue when markets are overheated and the signal is blunted

Apple and Alphabet held vast cash balances and low leverage for years despite large available tax shields, valuing the option to deploy cash opportunistically (acquisitions, R&D, buybacks during downturns) more than the tax savings they would get from levering up

LBOs exploit the tax shield and impose operating discipline through debt covenants; when the tax shield benefit exceeds the distress cost increase on a target firm, an LBO can create value, which is why private equity focuses on stable cash flow industries

class_schedule.topic: Working Capital Management and Short-Term Financing
learning_objectives: Compute the cash conversion cycle from days inventory, days receivables, and days payables and interpret the operating cash cycle
Evaluate credit policy decisions including credit standards, credit terms, and collection policy using marginal analysis
Choose appropriate short-term financing sources including commercial paper, lines of credit, factoring, and trade credit
Apply cash management techniques including target cash balances, lockbox systems, and short-term investment portfolios
readings: Brealey, Myers & Allen, Principles of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 29-30 - Working Capital Management, Short-Term Financial Planning
Ross, Westerfield & Jordan, Fundamentals of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 19-20 - Short-Term Finance and Planning, Cash and Liquidity Management
Berk & DeMarzo, Corporate Finance (5th ed.), Ch. 26-27 - Working Capital Management, Short-Term Financial Planning
Ross, Westerfield, Jaffe & Jordan, Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 26-27

lecture_notes.introduction: Long-term capital budgeting and capital structure get most of the textbook attention, but working capital management determines whether a firm survives long enough for those long-term decisions to matter. A profitable company can go bankrupt from a liquidity crisis, and many do. Dell built its business in the 1990s partly on a brutally efficient working capital model: customers paid up front (credit cards), suppliers were paid 60 days later, and inventory was kept at a few days rather than the weeks or months typical of competitors; Dell's cash conversion cycle was negative, meaning customers essentially financed Dell's operations rather than the other way around, and this liquidity advantage funded growth without equity dilution. Amazon achieved similar working capital efficiency in retail, again pushing the cash conversion cycle negative. By contrast, when retailers with long cash cycles hit demand slowdowns, their working capital requirements explode, cash dries up, and they end up in liquidity crises that force painful emergency financing. This session covers the three main elements of working capital management: cash (holding the right balance for liquidity without sacrificing return), receivables (deciding how much credit to extend to customers and how aggressively to collect), and inventory (balancing stock-out costs against carrying costs). We also cover short-term financing, the counterpart to working capital on the liability side: commercial paper for the largest investment-grade firms, committed bank lines of credit as a safety net, factoring and asset-based lending for firms with strong receivables, and trade credit from suppliers as a source of near-free financing if used within discount periods.
key_concepts: Operating cycle = days inventory outstanding (DIO) + days sales outstanding (DSO); the time from buying inventory to collecting cash from selling it
Cash conversion cycle (CCC) = DIO + DSO - days payables outstanding (DPO); the time the firm's own cash is tied up in operations

Negative cash conversion cycle: firms like Dell, Amazon, and Costco achieve negative CCC, meaning they sell and collect faster than they have to pay suppliers, effectively using supplier financing to fund operations
Credit policy: the decision to grant credit and the terms involved; tighter credit reduces bad debt but also reduces sales, and the optimal policy balances the two effects at the margin

Credit terms: '2/10 net 30' means 2% discount if paid within 10 days, otherwise full payment due in 30 days; the implicit annual interest rate for not taking the discount is (0.02/0.98) x (365/20) = 37.2%, extremely expensive borrowing

Collection policy: the aging of receivables (current, 30 days, 60 days, 90+) and the methods used to collect overdue accounts; aggressive collection recovers more but can damage customer relationships

Inventory management: economic order quantity (EOQ = sqrt(2 x D x S / H)) balances ordering costs against holding costs; just-in-time (JIT) minimizes inventory investment but requires reliable supply chains
Target cash balance: Baumol model and Miller-Orr model provide theoretical frameworks for minimum cash balances balancing transaction costs against opportunity cost of lost return

Short-term financing sources: commercial paper (large IG firms, 30-270 day unsecured notes), bank lines of credit (committed and uncommitted), asset-based lending (secured by receivables or inventory), factoring (selling receivables at a discount)

Trade credit: financing from suppliers in the form of delayed payment terms; effectively free if paid within the discount period but extremely expensive if the discount is foregone and payment is delayed
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compute Apple's cash conversion cycle using its 2023 10-K figures. Approximate numbers: inventory $6.3B, cost of goods sold $214B, DIO = (6.3 / 214) x 365 = 10.7 days. Accounts receivable $29.5B, revenue $383B, DSO = (29.5 / 383) x 365 = 28.1 days. Accounts payable $62B, COGS $214B, DPO = (62 / 214) x 365 = 105.8 days. CCC = 10.7 + 28.1 - 105.8 = -67 days. Apple's cash conversion cycle is dramatically negative: it collects cash from customers long before it has to pay suppliers, which is why Apple's working capital investment is negative and its cash balance grows from operations. Contrast with a traditional retailer: DIO = 60 days, DSO = 45 days, DPO = 35 days, CCC = +70 days; for every $1 billion of sales this retailer needs roughly $192 million in working capital tied up in operations. Discuss how Apple's negative cycle helped it avoid needing external financing during growth phases, while traditional retailers needed bank lines of credit that consumed management attention and interest expense.
setup_requirements: Excel working capital template with DIO, DSO, DPO, and CCC calculations
Most recent 10-Ks for Apple, Amazon, Dell, and a traditional retailer for comparison
Federal Reserve commercial paper rate data for cost of short-term financing benchmarks
Sample supplier invoice showing 2/10 net 30 discount terms for hands-on discount cost calculation

review.value: Every dollar tied up in inventory and receivables is a dollar that must be financed somehow, either with debt, equity, or supplier credit; reducing working capital frees up that capital for higher-return uses or reduces financing costs

2/10 net 30 implies a 37% annual interest rate for not taking the discount, one of the most expensive forms of borrowing if you can finance yourself otherwise; firms that routinely forego trade discounts are often implicitly borrowing at punitive rates because they lack access to cheaper financing

Amazon, Costco, and Apple have achieved negative cash conversion cycles by combining fast inventory turnover, quick customer payments, and patient suppliers; this is a durable structural advantage that funds growth without dilution and buffers against downturns

Only investment-grade firms with top credit ratings can consistently access the commercial paper market; firms that lose IG ratings are forced into more expensive bank lines of credit or asset-based loans, as happened to Ford and GM during the 2008 crisis

class_schedule.topic: Dividend Policy, Payout, and Course Synthesis
learning_objectives: Compare dividends and share repurchases as payout mechanisms and explain the tax and signaling differences
State and critique the Miller-Modigliani dividend irrelevance proposition and identify the frictions that make payout policy matter in practice
Analyze real payout decisions at Apple, Microsoft, Amazon, Boeing, and Berkshire Hathaway using the theoretical frameworks developed in the course
Synthesize the core concepts of business finance into a coherent framework for evaluating corporate financial decisions
readings: Brealey, Myers & Allen, Principles of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 16 - Payout Policy
Ross, Westerfield & Jordan, Fundamentals of Corporate Finance (13th ed.), Ch. 17 - Dividends and Payout Policy
Berk & DeMarzo, Corporate Finance (5th ed.), Ch. 17 - Payout Policy
Miller & Modigliani, 'Dividend Policy, Growth, and the Valuation of Shares' (1961), Journal of Business
Buffett, Berkshire Hathaway Annual Letter (2012) - on dividends, buybacks, and intrinsic value

lecture_notes.introduction: After a firm has made its investment decisions and determined its capital structure, it must decide what to do with any cash it does not need for operations, debt service, or positive-NPV investment: hold it on the balance sheet, pay it out as dividends, or repurchase shares. This decision, the payout decision, completes the trio of corporate finance decisions and is the subject of the closing session. Miller and Modigliani (1961) showed that, under perfect markets, payout policy is irrelevant, much like capital structure: a dollar paid as a dividend leaves the stock worth a dollar less, and shareholders can always create homemade dividends by selling shares or reinvest unwanted dividends back into the firm. But as with capital structure, real-world frictions make payout policy matter. Taxes create preferences: historically dividends were taxed at ordinary income rates and capital gains at lower rates, biasing firms toward buybacks; recent U.S. tax policy equalized rates, weakening but not eliminating the bias. Signaling creates strong effects: an unexpected dividend increase is typically met with a stock price increase (positive signal about future earnings), while a dividend cut is severely punished. When Boeing suspended its dividend during the 737 MAX crisis and COVID downturn in 2020, the stock dropped sharply in part because the suspension was read as a signal that the crisis was worse than Boeing had previously admitted. Agency considerations matter: Jensen (1986) argued that free cash flow in the hands of managers often gets wasted on empire building and poor acquisitions, so forced payouts through dividends or buybacks can create value by disciplining management. Apple is perhaps the clearest example of payout policy evolution: no dividends for most of its history, then the 2012 dividend initiation and massive buyback program under Tim Cook, which since 2013 has returned over $900 billion to shareholders combined, more than the market cap of most public companies. By contrast, Berkshire Hathaway has famously never paid a dividend, with Warren Buffett arguing that he and Charlie Munger could reinvest each retained dollar at higher returns than shareholders could earn elsewhere. This session applies the full course framework to these real decisions and then steps back for a synthesis: capital budgeting, capital structure, and payout policy are three facets of the single question finance answers, which is how to allocate capital to maximize long-run shareholder value.
key_concepts: Dividends: cash distributions to shareholders, typically paid quarterly; the dividend is declared by the board, with ex-dividend date, record date, and payment date forming the dividend timeline

Share repurchases: firms buy their own shares in the open market, through tender offers, or through private deals; reduces shares outstanding and increases EPS mechanically, and distributes cash to exiting shareholders while non-selling shareholders get increased ownership

Miller-Modigliani dividend irrelevance: in perfect markets, payout policy does not affect firm value because investors can create homemade payouts; assumes no taxes, no transaction costs, and no information asymmetry

Tax differentials: historically dividends were taxed as ordinary income while capital gains on buybacks could be deferred and taxed at lower rates; the Jobs and Growth Tax Relief Reconciliation Act of 2003 narrowed this gap to 15%, and the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act kept it in place

Dividend signaling (Lintner model): firms smooth dividends and are reluctant to cut them; an unexpected dividend increase signals confidence in sustainable future earnings, while a cut is a severe negative signal

Clientele effects: different investor groups prefer different payout policies; retirees often prefer dividend-paying stocks, while taxable high-income investors often prefer buybacks, and firms attract the clientele that matches their policy

Jensen's free cash flow hypothesis: managers with excess free cash flow tend to invest in empire-building or value-destroying acquisitions; forcing payouts through dividends or buybacks reduces this agency cost

Stock repurchase mechanics: a buyback of N shares at price P reduces the share count by N and cash by N x P, leaving remaining shares worth more because the same future cash flows are divided among fewer shareholders

Dividend vs buyback equivalence in perfect markets: MM would say a dollar paid as dividend and a dollar spent on a buyback are equivalent; frictions (taxes, information signals, flexibility) cause firms to choose one or the other

Three pillars synthesis: capital budgeting determines how capital is deployed into assets, capital structure determines how that capital is raised, payout policy determines what is done with excess cash, and all three are connected through the cost of capital and the cash flows of the firm
lecture_notes.demonstration: Synthesize the full course using Apple's decade of capital allocation from 2013 to 2023. In 2013, under pressure from activist investor Carl Icahn among others, Apple announced a major capital return program. Over the decade, Apple paid roughly $130 billion in dividends and repurchased approximately $600 billion in stock, reducing shares outstanding from around 26 billion to around 15 billion. This took each remaining dollar of earnings and divided it among a smaller base of shares, boosting EPS significantly. Apple funded a portion of this payout with debt issuance, borrowing at very low rates to avoid repatriating overseas cash that would have triggered U.S. tax (pre-Tax Cuts and Jobs Act). The result: the tax shield on the debt added value, the buybacks returned cash without the tax drag of dividends for most shareholders, the reduced share count amplified EPS growth from underlying business gains, and the signaling was positive throughout. Apply each piece of the course: capital budgeting (chip design, stores, services investment), capital structure (moderate leverage despite huge cash, using debt's tax shield), cost of capital (Apple's low beta and strong credit give it a WACC near 8%), payout policy (buybacks over dividends for tax efficiency, dividends for signaling and clientele), and synthesis (the combination produced exceptional shareholder returns without compromising financial flexibility or growth capacity). Contrast briefly with Boeing's 2020 dividend suspension, GE's 2018 dividend cut, and Berkshire's zero-dividend policy to show how firms with different circumstances rationally reach different conclusions.
setup_requirements: Apple 10-K filings from 2013 through 2023 showing dividend and buyback history
Warren Buffett's 2012 Berkshire Hathaway annual letter (dividend discussion)
Excel synthesis workbook connecting capital budgeting, capital structure, and payout modules
Final course reference sheet with all formulas from weeks 1 through 14 for review

review.key: Dividends Are Sticky, Buybacks Are Flexible
review.value: Lintner-style smoothing makes firms reluctant to cut dividends because of the negative signal, so dividends become quasi-fixed commitments; buybacks are flexible and can be scaled up or down year to year without signaling problems, which is one of the main reasons buybacks have grown to dominate payout since the 1980s

When Boeing suspended its dividend in March 2020 amid the 737 MAX crisis and COVID demand collapse, the stock fell sharply; the suspension was read as a signal that the crisis was worse than management had previously admitted, illustrating how payout decisions transmit information to markets

Berkshire Hathaway has never paid a dividend because Buffett argues that every dollar retained has earned more than a dollar of market value over the long run; the rule he states in his 2012 letter is to retain only if the retained dollar creates more than a dollar of market value, otherwise pay it out, and Berkshire's track record has historically satisfied this test

Capital budgeting, capital structure, and payout policy are connected through a single framework: invest only in positive NPV projects, finance at the lowest WACC consistent with flexibility and risk, and return excess cash to shareholders when you cannot reinvest it at or above the cost of capital; this three-line summary is the entire course, and every topic we covered is an implementation detail of this principle

quizzes.topic: Financial Analysis and Time Value of Money I

questions.question: A company reports: Net income $40 million, Sales $500 million, Total assets $400 million, Total equity $200 million. Perform a DuPont decomposition and state the three component ratios plus ROE. Then explain which component a management team should focus on improving if the firm's main weakness is operating efficiency rather than margins or leverage.
questions.answer:
DuPont decomposition: ROE = Net Profit Margin x Asset Turnover x Equity Multiplier.

Net Profit Margin = Net Income / Sales = 40 / 500 = 8.0%
Asset Turnover = Sales / Total Assets = 500 / 400 = 1.25
Equity Multiplier = Total Assets / Total Equity = 400 / 200 = 2.0

ROE = 8.0% x 1.25 x 2.0 = 20.0%

Verification: ROE = Net Income / Equity = 40 / 200 = 20.0%. Confirmed.

If the firm's main weakness is operating efficiency (not margins, not leverage), the component to improve is Asset Turnover (Sales / Total Assets). Operating efficiency in DuPont terms is how much sales the firm generates per dollar of assets: low turnover means the firm has too many assets relative to its sales, typically due to excess inventory, excess receivables, idle plant capacity, or bloated real estate. Management levers include reducing inventory through JIT practices, tightening receivables collection, selling underutilized fixed assets, or growing sales on the existing asset base. At 1.25 asset turnover, the firm generates $1.25 of sales per dollar of assets, which is below the 2.0-3.0 range of efficient retailers like Walmart or Costco, suggesting real room to improve. A move from 1.25 to 1.75 turnover, holding margin and leverage constant, would raise ROE from 20.0% to 8.0% x 1.75 x 2.0 = 28.0%, an 8-percentage-point ROE gain without any change in pricing or leverage.

rubric.key: Correct calculation of all three DuPont components (margin, turnover, equity multiplier)
Correct ROE = 20% with verification
Correct identification of Asset Turnover as efficiency component with practical improvement levers

questions.question: You invest $25,000 today at an annual return of 8%, compounded annually. How much will it be worth in 30 years? How much would it be worth if instead of 8% annually the rate were 8% nominal compounded quarterly? Show all calculations.
questions.answer:
Part 1: Annual compounding.
FV = PV x (1 + r)^n = 25,000 x (1.08)^30
(1.08)^30 = 10.0627
FV = 25,000 x 10.0627 = $251,566.42

Part 2: Quarterly compounding at 8% nominal annual.
Quarterly rate = 0.08 / 4 = 0.02
Number of quarters = 30 x 4 = 120
FV = 25,000 x (1.02)^120
(1.02)^120 = 10.7652
FV = 25,000 x 10.7652 = $269,129.72

Difference: $269,129.72 - $251,566.42 = $17,563.30

The quarterly-compounded investment grows to $17,563 more because of more frequent compounding. In effective annual rate terms, 8% compounded quarterly is (1.02)^4 - 1 = 8.2432% EAR, so over 30 years this compounds to a larger total. The lesson: when comparing investment products, always convert stated rates to effective annual rates to make apples-to-apples comparisons, because a seemingly identical 8% stated rate can produce different outcomes depending on compounding frequency.

rubric.key: Correct annual compounding result ($251,566 or very close)
Correct quarterly compounding result ($269,130 or very close)
Correctly identifies EAR effect as the source of the difference

questions.question: A firm has current ratio 1.1, quick ratio 0.4, inventory turnover 4, and days sales outstanding 85. The industry medians are current ratio 1.8, quick ratio 1.0, inventory turnover 10, and DSO 40. Identify three red flags in this firm's working capital position and explain what each suggests about the business.
questions.answer:
Red flag 1: Low quick ratio (0.4 vs industry 1.0). The quick ratio excludes inventory, so it measures the firm's ability to meet short-term obligations without selling inventory. A quick ratio of 0.4 means the firm can cover only 40 cents of every dollar of current liabilities from cash and receivables alone; if suppliers or lenders called in their claims, the firm would be forced to liquidate inventory, which typically happens at a discount and may not complete fast enough. Combined with the low current ratio (1.1), the firm is close to the line where short-term liabilities exceed liquid assets, a serious liquidity risk.

Red flag 2: Low inventory turnover (4 vs industry 10). Industry firms turn inventory 10 times per year (roughly 36 days on the shelf), but this firm turns only 4 times per year (91 days on the shelf). Slow inventory indicates either (a) declining demand with inventory accumulating faster than it is sold, (b) obsolete inventory that may need to be written down, or (c) poor inventory management with oversized purchasing. Each possibility points to future margin pressure and potential write-offs. The slow inventory also ties up cash, contributing to the liquidity problem in red flag 1.

Red flag 3: High days sales outstanding (85 vs industry 40). The firm takes 85 days on average to collect receivables, more than double the industry. This suggests either (a) the firm has extended generous credit terms to drive sales (aggressive sales practices that may lead to bad debt), (b) customers are struggling to pay, signaling weak customer credit quality, or (c) the firm has failed to enforce its collection policy. Extended DSO also ties up working capital and may foreshadow an increase in bad debt expense if the receivables are not ultimately collectible.

Connected interpretation: All three flags point in the same direction. The firm has slow-moving inventory, slow-paying customers, and tight liquidity; this pattern is characteristic of a firm in financial distress or heading toward it. A finance team should investigate whether earnings are being supported by aggressive revenue recognition and whether the firm can service its debt through the next downturn.

rubric.key: Red flag 1: low quick ratio correctly interpreted as liquidity weakness
Red flag 2: slow inventory turnover correctly interpreted as demand or obsolescence problem
Red flag 3: high DSO correctly interpreted as credit policy or customer quality problem
Synthesis connecting the flags into a coherent diagnosis

questions.question: Calculate the price of a 10-year bond with $1,000 face value, 5% annual coupon (paid semi-annually, so $25 per six months), and a yield to maturity of 6% (3% per semi-annual period). Then state whether the bond trades at a discount or premium and explain why.
questions.answer:
Inputs: Face = $1,000, semi-annual coupon = $25, semi-annual YTM = 3%, periods = 20.

Price = PV of coupon annuity + PV of face value.

PV of coupon annuity:
Annuity factor = [1 - (1.03)^(-20)] / 0.03
(1.03)^20 = 1.80611
(1.03)^(-20) = 1 / 1.80611 = 0.55368
Annuity factor = (1 - 0.55368) / 0.03 = 0.44632 / 0.03 = 14.8775
PV of coupons = 25 x 14.8775 = $371.94

PV of face value:
PV = 1,000 / (1.03)^20 = 1,000 / 1.80611 = $553.68

Bond price = 371.94 + 553.68 = $925.62

The bond trades at a DISCOUNT ($925.62 < $1,000 face value).

Reason: The coupon rate (5%) is below the yield to maturity (6%), so the bond pays less cash than investors require for bonds of this risk and maturity. To make a buyer indifferent, the bond must offer a capital gain at maturity (bought at $925.62, paid $1,000 at maturity), which combined with the coupons provides the 6% yield investors demand. This is the inverse price-yield relationship: when required yields rise above the coupon rate, price falls below par; when they fall below the coupon rate, price rises above par.

rubric.key: Correct PV of coupons using semi-annual annuity formula ($371.94 or very close)
Correct PV of face value ($553.68 or very close)
Correct total price $925.62 with discount identification and explanation of why coupon < YTM implies discount

questions.question: Use the constant-growth dividend discount model to value a stock with the following characteristics: next year's expected dividend $2.00, dividend growth rate 5% per year indefinitely, required return 8%. Then recompute the price if investors demand a 10% return instead and interpret the sensitivity.
questions.answer:
Part 1: r = 8%, g = 5%.
Gordon growth formula: P0 = D1 / (r - g)
P0 = 2.00 / (0.08 - 0.05) = 2.00 / 0.03 = $66.67

Part 2: r = 10%, g = 5%.
P0 = 2.00 / (0.10 - 0.05) = 2.00 / 0.05 = $40.00

Sensitivity interpretation: A 2 percentage point increase in required return (from 8% to 10%) reduced the stock price by 40%, from $66.67 to $40.00. This enormous sensitivity arises because the Gordon formula has (r - g) in the denominator, so small changes in the spread between required return and growth dramatically shift the valuation. The stock with r = 8%, g = 5% has a spread of 3%, and the stock with r = 10%, g = 5% has a spread of 5%, a 67% increase in the spread that translates directly into a 40% price reduction.

Practical lesson: Valuations from the Gordon model are hyper-sensitive to the assumed growth rate and required return. An analyst who changes the growth rate from 4% to 6% holding r = 8% fixed moves the stock price from $50 to $100, a 100% change. This is why experienced analysts report valuation ranges rather than point estimates and why high-growth stocks have such volatile prices: small changes in market expectations about future growth or risk move valuations dramatically.

rubric.key: Correct price at r = 8% ($66.67)
Correct price at r = 10% ($40.00)
Correct sensitivity interpretation with percentage change and explanation of r-g denominator effect

questions.question: A bond has modified duration of 8.2 years and a current price of $1,020. Interest rates rise by 0.75% (75 basis points). Estimate the approximate new price using modified duration. Then discuss the limitation of this estimate relative to the true price change.
questions.answer:
Modified duration approximation: percent price change approximately = -modified duration x change in yield.

Percent price change = -8.2 x 0.0075 = -0.0615 = -6.15%

Estimated new price = $1,020 x (1 - 0.0615) = $1,020 x 0.9385 = $957.27

Estimated price drop: $1,020 - $957.27 = $62.73

Limitation: Modified duration is a linear approximation of the price-yield relationship. The actual price-yield curve is convex (curves upward), meaning: (a) when yields rise, the true price falls by LESS than the duration estimate, and (b) when yields fall, the true price rises by MORE than the duration estimate. This curvature is called convexity. For small yield changes (a few basis points), the linear approximation is nearly exact. For larger changes (75 bps is moderate), the approximation is still reasonable but slightly underestimates the true price. A more accurate estimate uses the convexity adjustment:

Percent price change = -modified duration x delta y + 0.5 x convexity x (delta y)^2

The convexity term is always positive (because convexity is positive for standard option-free bonds), so it partially offsets the loss when yields rise and adds to the gain when yields fall. Without the convexity term, we systematically overestimate losses and underestimate gains from rate moves, a bias that becomes more pronounced for large rate changes and long-duration bonds.

In this specific case, the true new price is probably slightly above $957.27, perhaps around $960, because of the convexity benefit during a 75 bp rate rise. The duration estimate is useful for quick analysis but should be supplemented with a full bond pricing formula for precision.

rubric.key: Correct application of modified duration formula to compute percent price change (-6.15%)
Correct estimated new price ($957.27 or very close)
Identifies convexity as the source of the linear approximation error with correct direction (underestimates true price when yields rise)

questions.question: A portfolio holds 60% Stock A (expected return 14%, standard deviation 25%) and 40% Stock B (expected return 8%, standard deviation 15%). The correlation between A and B is 0.3. Calculate the portfolio's expected return and standard deviation. Then state whether the portfolio is less volatile than a weighted average of the individual volatilities and explain why.
questions.answer:
Portfolio expected return:
E(Rp) = w_A x E(R_A) + w_B x E(R_B)
E(Rp) = 0.6 x 14% + 0.4 x 8% = 8.4% + 3.2% = 11.6%

Portfolio variance:
Var(Rp) = w_A^2 x sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 x sigma_B^2 + 2 x w_A x w_B x rho x sigma_A x sigma_B
Var(Rp) = (0.6)^2 x (0.25)^2 + (0.4)^2 x (0.15)^2 + 2 x 0.6 x 0.4 x 0.3 x 0.25 x 0.15
Var(Rp) = 0.36 x 0.0625 + 0.16 x 0.0225 + 2 x 0.6 x 0.4 x 0.3 x 0.0375
Var(Rp) = 0.0225 + 0.0036 + 0.0054
Var(Rp) = 0.0315

Portfolio standard deviation = sqrt(0.0315) = 0.1775 = 17.75%

Weighted average of individual volatilities:
0.6 x 25% + 0.4 x 15% = 15% + 6% = 21%

The portfolio's standard deviation (17.75%) is LOWER than the weighted average of individual standard deviations (21%).

Reason: This is the diversification benefit in action. When correlation is less than +1, the portfolio variance formula includes a cross term (2 x w_A x w_B x rho x sigma_A x sigma_B) that is smaller than it would be if rho were 1. Specifically, with rho = 1, the portfolio SD would equal exactly 21% (the weighted average). With rho = 0.3, the cross term is only 30% of its maximum value, so the portfolio variance and SD are lower. The general principle: any correlation below 1 creates a diversification benefit, and the lower the correlation, the larger the benefit, reaching its maximum when correlation is -1 (where a combination can theoretically achieve zero volatility). This is why portfolio managers seek uncorrelated or negatively correlated assets, because they lower risk without necessarily sacrificing expected return.

rubric.key: Correct expected return calculation (11.6%)
Correct portfolio variance calculation using the three-term formula (0.0315)
Correct standard deviation (17.75%) and correct identification that it is below the weighted average (21%)
Correct explanation referencing correlation < 1 and the cross-term in the variance formula

questions.question: Using CAPM, calculate the required return for a stock with beta 1.4, given a risk-free rate of 4% and an equity risk premium of 5.5%. Then explain what would happen to the required return if the firm increased its leverage, and which direction beta would move.
questions.answer:
CAPM formula: E(R) = R_f + beta x (equity risk premium)

E(R) = 4% + 1.4 x 5.5%
E(R) = 4% + 7.7%
E(R) = 11.7%

Effect of increased leverage: If the firm increases its debt-to-equity ratio, the equity beta will INCREASE. This follows from the levered/unlevered beta relationship:

beta_L = beta_U x [1 + (1 - T_c) x D/E]

Where beta_L is the levered (equity) beta, beta_U is the unlevered (asset) beta, T_c is the corporate tax rate, and D/E is the debt-to-equity ratio. As D/E rises, the multiplier [1 + (1 - T_c) x D/E] grows, so beta_L grows, so the required return on equity grows.

Intuition: Debt is senior to equity in the claim waterfall. When the firm increases leverage, the fixed interest obligations take priority over residual cash flows to equity, so equity holders receive a more volatile stream of leftover cash flows. More volatile equity returns means higher beta means higher required return, consistent with MM Proposition II which states R_e = R_a + (R_a - R_d) x (D/E) in the no-tax case. Quantitatively, if this firm's unlevered beta is 1.0 and the D/E rises from 0 to 1.0 at a 25% tax rate, the levered beta becomes 1.0 x [1 + 0.75 x 1.0] = 1.75, and the required return becomes 4% + 1.75 x 5.5% = 13.625%, a significant increase from the original 11.7%.

Important caveat: While the cost of equity rises with leverage, the WACC may remain relatively flat because the increased cost of equity is offset by the larger share of (cheaper, tax-shielded) debt in the capital structure. In the Modigliani-Miller world without distress costs, WACC is exactly constant; in the real world, WACC has a U-shape with a minimum at the optimal capital structure.

rubric.key: Correct CAPM calculation (11.7%)
Correct direction: beta and required return increase with leverage
Correct explanation using the levered/unlevered beta formula or MM Proposition II intuition

questions.question: A company has $600 million market value of equity with beta 1.2, $400 million market value of debt with YTM 6.5%, and a 25% corporate tax rate. The risk-free rate is 4.5% and the market risk premium is 5.5%. Calculate the company's WACC. Show each step.
questions.answer:
Step 1: Market value weights.
V = E + D = 600 + 400 = $1,000 million
E/V = 600 / 1,000 = 0.60 = 60%
D/V = 400 / 1,000 = 0.40 = 40%

Step 2: Cost of equity using CAPM.
R_e = R_f + beta x (market risk premium)
R_e = 4.5% + 1.2 x 5.5%
R_e = 4.5% + 6.6%
R_e = 11.1%

Step 3: After-tax cost of debt.
R_d = 6.5% (pretax YTM)
R_d x (1 - T_c) = 6.5% x (1 - 0.25) = 6.5% x 0.75 = 4.875%

Step 4: Weighted average cost of capital.
WACC = (E/V) x R_e + (D/V) x R_d x (1 - T_c)
WACC = 0.60 x 11.1% + 0.40 x 4.875%
WACC = 6.66% + 1.95%
WACC = 8.61%

Interpretation: The firm's overall cost of capital is 8.61%. This is the hurdle rate that any new project of average firm risk must clear to create value. If the firm invests in a project with expected return 9%, the project creates value; if it invests in a project with expected return 8%, the project destroys value even though the return is positive, because the cost of financing exceeds the return. The tax shield on debt contributes meaningfully: without the tax shield, after-tax cost of debt would equal pretax cost of debt (6.5%), and WACC would rise to 0.60 x 11.1% + 0.40 x 6.5% = 6.66% + 2.60% = 9.26%. The tax shield on debt saves 65 basis points of WACC, which compounded over many years of investments adds substantial value.

rubric.key: Correct weights (E/V = 60%, D/V = 40%)
Correct cost of equity using CAPM (11.1%)
Correct after-tax cost of debt (4.875%)
Correct final WACC (8.61%) with interpretation as hurdle rate

questions.question: Evaluate a project with the following cash flows at a 10% discount rate: Year 0 = -$200,000, Year 1 = $60,000, Year 2 = $80,000, Year 3 = $90,000, Year 4 = $70,000. Compute NPV, payback period, and determine whether you would accept the project.
questions.answer:
NPV calculation at r = 10%:
Year 0: -200,000 / (1.10)^0 = -200,000
Year 1: 60,000 / (1.10)^1 = 60,000 / 1.10 = 54,545.45
Year 2: 80,000 / (1.10)^2 = 80,000 / 1.21 = 66,115.70
Year 3: 90,000 / (1.10)^3 = 90,000 / 1.331 = 67,618.33
Year 4: 70,000 / (1.10)^4 = 70,000 / 1.4641 = 47,810.25

NPV = -200,000 + 54,545.45 + 66,115.70 + 67,618.33 + 47,810.25
NPV = -200,000 + 236,089.73
NPV = $36,089.73

Payback period calculation (using undiscounted cash flows):
End of Year 1: cumulative = 60,000; still $140,000 to recover
End of Year 2: cumulative = 140,000; still $60,000 to recover
End of Year 3: cumulative = 230,000; investment fully recovered during Year 3

Payback = 2 + (60,000 / 90,000) = 2 + 0.667 = 2.67 years

Decision: ACCEPT the project.

Justification: NPV is positive ($36,090), meaning the project is expected to create $36,090 of present-value wealth for shareholders after accounting for the time value and opportunity cost of capital. NPV is the theoretically correct decision criterion, so positive NPV means accept. Payback of 2.67 years is also informative: the firm recovers its initial investment before the project ends, so the project has good liquidity characteristics as well. Both criteria support acceptance. Note that NPV is the primary justification because payback alone ignores the magnitude of post-payback cash flows and the time value of money, so payback should never override NPV when they disagree.

rubric.key: Correct present value of each individual cash flow
Correct NPV = $36,089.73 (or very close)
Correct payback period = 2.67 years
Correct decision (accept) with NPV-based justification

questions.question:
A firm is considering two mutually exclusive projects at a 10% cost of capital:

Project A: Initial investment $100,000, single cash inflow of $140,000 in Year 1. IRR = 40%.
Project B: Initial investment $400,000, single cash inflow of $520,000 in Year 1. IRR = 30%.

Which project should the firm accept and why? Show the NPVs and explain the IRR-NPV conflict.
questions.answer:
NPV of Project A:
NPV_A = -100,000 + 140,000 / 1.10 = -100,000 + 127,272.73 = $27,272.73

NPV of Project B:
NPV_B = -400,000 + 520,000 / 1.10 = -400,000 + 472,727.27 = $72,727.27

IRR ranking: A > B (40% > 30%)
NPV ranking: B > A ($72,727 > $27,273)

Decision: ACCEPT Project B.

Explanation of the IRR-NPV conflict: The two criteria rank the projects in opposite orders, and this type of conflict is common for mutually exclusive projects of different sizes. IRR is a percentage measure that favors smaller projects with higher percent returns, regardless of absolute dollar value created. NPV is a dollar measure that correctly reflects the absolute wealth added to shareholders.

Project A earns a higher percentage return (40%) but on a smaller base, creating only $27,273 of value. Project B earns a lower percentage return (30%) but on a much larger base, creating $72,727 of value, which is 2.67 times more wealth than Project A. Since the firm can only choose one (they are mutually exclusive), the firm should maximize total value created, which means choosing Project B.

The theoretical flaw with IRR here: IRR implicitly assumes the firm can reinvest the $100,000 saved by choosing A instead of B at the IRR of 40%, but in practice the firm can only reinvest at its cost of capital (10%). At the true reinvestment rate, the $100,000 saved by choosing A grows to only $110,000 in Year 1, while Project B's $400,000 investment generates an extra $380,000 in Year 1 cash flows compared to A's $140,000. The incremental cash flow (B minus A) has its own NPV, which is positive at 10%, confirming B is the correct choice.

General rule: When NPV and IRR conflict for mutually exclusive projects, always follow NPV.

rubric.key: Correct NPV calculations for both projects
Correct identification of the NPV-IRR conflict (IRR ranks A higher, NPV ranks B higher)
Correct decision to accept Project B
Correct explanation involving scale effect and the incorrect reinvestment rate assumption implicit in IRR

questions.question: A firm is considering increasing its leverage from 20% debt to 50% debt. Currently its WACC is 9% with cost of equity 10% and after-tax cost of debt 5%. Under perfect Modigliani-Miller conditions (no taxes, no distress costs), what would happen to the cost of equity, the cost of debt, and the WACC? Under real-world conditions with taxes and distress costs, how would your answer differ?
questions.answer:
Under perfect MM conditions (no taxes, no distress costs):

Cost of equity RISES: MM Proposition II states R_e = R_a + (R_a - R_d) x (D/E), where R_a is the unlevered (asset) cost of capital. As D/E rises from 0.25 to 1.0, the cost of equity rises linearly. Equity holders bear more risk because the senior claim of debt has grown, leaving a more volatile residual for equity, so they demand higher returns.

Cost of debt: In the purest MM world, the cost of debt is assumed constant across leverage levels because debt is riskless. In a slightly more realistic version, once leverage grows enough to make bankruptcy possible, cost of debt rises too as credit risk increases.

WACC: Under pure MM without taxes, WACC is CONSTANT regardless of capital structure. This is MM Proposition I restated: the firm's overall cost of capital depends only on its business risk (asset risk), not on how the claims are divided between debt and equity. The cheaper debt in the mix is exactly offset by the higher cost of equity, leaving WACC unchanged at 9%.

Under real-world conditions with taxes and distress costs:

Cost of equity still RISES with leverage for the same risk-shifting reason, though the magnitude is different because of the tax shield.

Cost of debt starts LOW (tax shield benefit) but RISES AT HIGH LEVERAGE as lenders demand a credit risk premium for the increased bankruptcy probability.

WACC has a U-SHAPE:
- At low leverage: WACC FALLS as more debt is added, because the tax shield on debt exceeds any distress cost effect.
- At high leverage: WACC RISES because distress costs become significant and overwhelm the tax shield benefit.
- Somewhere in between is the OPTIMAL capital structure, where marginal tax shield equals marginal distress cost and WACC reaches its minimum.

For the specific move from 20% to 50% debt, the direction depends on where this firm sits on the U-curve. For most firms, 50% is still below the distress-cost inflection point, so WACC would likely decrease slightly (say from 9.0% to 8.5%-8.7%) as more tax shield is captured without material distress costs yet.

Bottom line: MM without frictions says capital structure does not matter; trade-off theory with frictions says it matters and there is an optimal level, beyond which additional leverage destroys rather than creates value.

rubric.key: Correct MM result: cost of equity rises, WACC constant, under pure MM with no taxes
Correct real-world result: WACC has a U-shape with an optimal interior leverage ratio
Correct identification of the tradeoff between tax shield and distress costs

questions.question: A firm has days inventory outstanding 45, days sales outstanding 50, and days payables outstanding 40. Compute the operating cycle and the cash conversion cycle. Then explain the practical meaning of the result and compare it to a firm with a negative cash conversion cycle.
questions.answer:
Operating cycle = Days Inventory Outstanding + Days Sales Outstanding
Operating cycle = 45 + 50 = 95 days

Cash conversion cycle = Operating cycle - Days Payables Outstanding
Cash conversion cycle = 95 - 40 = 55 days

Alternatively: CCC = DIO + DSO - DPO = 45 + 50 - 40 = 55 days.

Interpretation: The operating cycle of 95 days means it takes the firm 95 days from the time it acquires inventory until the time it collects cash from the customer who eventually buys that inventory (45 days to sell + 50 days to collect). Over that 95-day period, the firm has its own capital tied up in inventory and then in receivables. However, the firm delays payment to its own suppliers for 40 days on average, so supplier credit effectively funds the first 40 days of the operating cycle. The remaining 55 days (the cash conversion cycle) is the period during which the firm's OWN cash is tied up in operations, and this is what must be financed with either equity, long-term debt, or short-term credit like a bank line.

Practical meaning: For every $1 million of daily cost of operations, roughly $55 million of permanent working capital is tied up. Reducing CCC by, say, 15 days through faster collection or better inventory management would free $15 million per $1 million daily operations for alternative uses.

Comparison with negative CCC firm: A firm with negative CCC (like Apple, Amazon, or Dell in its prime) collects cash from customers BEFORE it has to pay suppliers. Customers fund operations rather than the other way around, and the firm actually generates cash from growth instead of consuming it. This is a major structural competitive advantage: it allows the firm to grow rapidly without needing outside financing, to weather downturns without running out of cash, and to deploy excess cash into investments or buybacks. Firms with positive CCC must borrow or dilute to grow; firms with negative CCC can grow on retained cash alone. The competitive gap between these two models is enormous, which is why operating efficiency and working capital management are strategic, not merely tactical, concerns.

rubric.key: Correct operating cycle (95 days)
Correct cash conversion cycle (55 days)
Correct interpretation connecting CCC to working capital investment requirement
Correct contrast with negative CCC firms and its competitive implications

questions.question: Microsoft initiated a regular dividend in 2003 and has since combined dividends, buybacks, and moderate debt. Apple did the same in 2012. Using pecking order theory and signaling theory, explain why these firms made these shifts at those specific points in their corporate evolution.
questions.answer:
Pecking order perspective: Both Microsoft and Apple generate enormous internal cash flows (Microsoft roughly $60 billion CFO today, Apple roughly $100 billion CFO today). Pecking order theory predicts that firms use internal funds first, then debt, then equity. As these firms matured, their positive-NPV investment opportunities grew more slowly than their cash generation, so cash accumulated on balance sheets: Microsoft had roughly $50 billion of cash by 2003, and Apple had over $100 billion by 2012. Holding unused cash creates agency problems (Jensen free cash flow concerns: managers might waste it on poor acquisitions) and represents inefficient capital allocation because the cash earns only short-term interest rates while equity investors demand higher returns. When internal cash flow persistently exceeds reinvestment needs, the pecking order has only one answer left: return the cash to shareholders.

Signaling perspective: Initiating a dividend is a strong positive signal. It tells the market that management is confident the firm's cash flows are sustainable and that growth opportunities requiring the cash have diminished. Both Microsoft in 2003 and Apple in 2012 were at stages where their core businesses had matured into stable cash generators (Microsoft's Windows/Office franchise by 2003, Apple's iPhone ecosystem by 2012), but the reinvestment needs had begun to plateau. The dividend initiation was a credible commitment to return cash, because dividends are sticky and hard to cut without a negative signal later.

Why specifically these timings: (1) Microsoft's 2003 initiation came after the antitrust case had resolved and the dot-com bust had removed growth expectations, leaving a firm with stable high-margin cash flows and too much cash; Bill Gates and the board effectively said, 'we cannot find enough good investments for this cash, so we will return it.' (2) Apple's 2012 initiation came two years after the iPad launch and during the iPhone 4S/5 era, when quarterly revenue was rapidly growing but cash was growing faster, creating a $100B+ cash hoard; Tim Cook, less attached to the Steve Jobs tradition of cash hoarding, responded to investor pressure (notably from Carl Icahn and David Einhorn) and initiated the dividend plus a massive buyback.

Additional factor - buyback preference: Both firms have relied more heavily on buybacks than dividends because buybacks are more flexible (scalable year to year without signaling problems) and, under U.S. tax law, generally more tax-efficient for taxable shareholders. The combination of a modest dividend (for signaling and dividend-seeking clienteles) plus large buybacks (for flexibility and tax efficiency) has become the dominant payout model for mature profitable tech firms, with Alphabet, Meta, and others adopting similar structures in 2024.

rubric.key: Correct pecking order interpretation connecting accumulated cash to payout decision
Correct signaling interpretation connecting dividend initiation to maturity and confidence
Specific timing explanation for both firms with concrete business context
Correct explanation of buyback preference and tax-efficiency rationale

questions.question: A financial analyst says: 'The project has positive NPV, so we should accept it.' Your senior manager responds: 'But the project increases our debt load, which will raise our cost of equity through higher leverage, and we should include that effect in our decision.' Whose reasoning is correct and why? Reference specific course concepts.
questions.answer:
The financial analyst is correct, and the senior manager is double-counting the leverage effect. Here is why.

The project NPV calculation should be performed using the firm's WACC (or a project-specific WACC if risk differs from the average project). WACC already embodies the current capital structure's cost of equity and after-tax cost of debt, weighted appropriately. By discounting project cash flows at the WACC, the NPV correctly reflects the blended opportunity cost of capital for the resources that will fund the project.

If the manager were right, we would need to separately penalize the project for the increased cost of equity caused by its financing. But this would double-count because:

1. The project's initial investment is already treated as a Year 0 cash outflow, capturing the cost of the capital deployed.

2. The discount rate (WACC) already accounts for the required return on both debt and equity components of financing, including the fact that as more debt is added, equity becomes riskier.

3. In the Modigliani-Miller framework, WACC is constant across capital structures (ignoring taxes and distress), so changing leverage does not actually change the hurdle rate. With taxes, WACC declines slightly with leverage up to the optimal point because of the tax shield, which would actually make the project MORE attractive, not less.

4. Any change in the firm's cost of equity due to the additional leverage is exactly offset by the lower weight of equity and higher weight of (cheaper after-tax) debt in the new WACC, leaving total capital cost approximately unchanged.

The senior manager's reasoning confuses the cost of the financing decision with the cost of the investment decision. These two decisions are separable in the classical Fisher separation theorem framework: first decide which projects to accept based on NPV at the appropriate hurdle rate, then decide the optimal capital structure, and the two decisions do not need to be jointly optimized in most cases.

The only legitimate situation where the financing decision affects the investment decision is when the project would push the firm past its optimal capital structure into a region of significant distress costs, OR when the project is so large that it materially changes the firm's risk profile (a pivot acquisition, for instance). In normal capital budgeting for average-sized projects, the correct approach is:

1. Estimate incremental after-tax cash flows.
2. Discount at the current WACC (or project-specific WACC).
3. Accept if NPV > 0.
4. Separately manage the capital structure over time to stay near the target debt ratio.

So the analyst is right: positive NPV means accept. The manager's concern is valid as a general principle about leverage costs but is already embedded in the WACC and should not be applied as a separate deduction.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies the analyst as right and explains why the manager is double-counting
Correctly explains that WACC already incorporates cost of equity and cost of debt under the current capital structure
References Modigliani-Miller or Fisher separation to justify separating investment and financing decisions

major_assignments.title: Comprehensive Valuation Report: Public Company DCF and Relative Valuation
major_assignments.description: Select a publicly traded U.S. company from a list of approved candidates (e.g., Starbucks, Nike, Target, Costco, Microsoft, Visa, Johnson & Johnson) and produce a full valuation report using both the discounted cash flow (DCF) approach with the dividend discount model or free cash flow valuation, and relative valuation using at least three multiples. The report must begin with a financial statement analysis, justify all assumptions, include sensitivity analysis on key inputs, and conclude with a buy/hold/sell recommendation relative to the current market price. This assignment integrates financial statement analysis, time value of money, stock valuation, and risk-return concepts from weeks 1-6 into a single applied deliverable of the kind produced daily on the sell side and buy side of Wall Street.
objectives: Apply financial statement analysis and ratio comparison to diagnose a real company's financial health
Build a multi-stage dividend discount model or free cash flow valuation with explicit growth assumptions and terminal value
Execute relative valuation using P/E, EV/EBITDA, P/B, and P/S multiples with properly selected comparables
Perform sensitivity analysis on key inputs (growth rate, required return, terminal multiple) and present results as a valuation range
deliverables: Company overview (1 page) with business description, industry, recent events, and stock performance chart over 5 years
Financial statement analysis (2 pages) with 5 years of common-size statements, key ratios, DuPont decomposition, and earnings quality red flags
DCF valuation (3 pages) with explicit 5-year forecast, terminal value calculation, discount rate derivation via CAPM, and sensitivity table
Relative valuation (2 pages) with 5 comparable company multiples (P/E, EV/EBITDA, P/B, P/S, PEG), justification of peer selection, and implied value
Investment recommendation (1 page) synthesizing both valuations, identifying the key risks and catalysts, and stating a buy/hold/sell decision with target price and holding horizon
Excel model file containing all calculations, historical data, projections, WACC derivation, comparable company data, and sensitivity tables, fully linked and audit-ready

criteria: DCF model is correctly constructed with explicit forecast period, terminal value, and proper discounting
WACC derivation uses CAPM correctly with well-justified risk-free rate, beta, and equity risk premium
Comparable company selection is appropriate with firms of similar industry, size, growth, and risk profile
All financial ratios are computed correctly and match footnotes in 10-K disclosures
Excel model is well-organized with clear separation of inputs, calculations, and outputs
Charts and tables are professionally formatted with consistent styling, clear labels, and appropriate axes
Written report follows investment research formatting with executive summary, sections, and conclusions
All exhibits have captions and source citations from 10-K, 10-Q, analyst reports, or Bloomberg
Sensitivity analysis identifies the 2-3 most important value drivers and presents a reasonable valuation range
Comparison of DCF and relative valuation is honest, with discussion of why values differ and which to trust
Recommendation is supported by the valuation work and connects to identified risks and catalysts
Target price is defensible from the valuation analysis rather than arbitrary
All assumptions are explicitly stated and justified with reference to company guidance, industry trends, or historical data
Sources are cited for every number used in the model (page references to 10-K, URLs for market data)
Limitations of the analysis are honestly discussed in the conclusion
Professional writing quality throughout with clear structure and minimal grammatical errors
Complete DCF valuation with CAPM-derived discount rate and explicit terminal value
Relative valuation with well-selected comparables and multiple measures
Sensitivity analysis covering the 2-3 most important value drivers
Professional written report with defensible buy/hold/sell recommendation

major_assignments.title: Capital Budgeting Case Study: New Product Line Investment Decision
major_assignments.description: Evaluate a major capital investment decision for a mid-size manufacturing firm considering a new product line. The case provides operating assumptions, initial investment requirements, expected revenues and costs, depreciation schedules, tax rates, working capital requirements, and salvage values. Students must build a full pro-forma cash flow model, compute NPV, IRR, MIRR, payback period, discounted payback, and profitability index, perform sensitivity and scenario analysis, and write a recommendation memo to the CFO. The deliverable must include a proper WACC derivation for the firm and discuss whether the project-specific risk profile justifies a different hurdle rate. This assignment mirrors the capital budgeting analysis performed in corporate finance departments and private equity investment committees.
objectives: Build a complete incremental cash flow model from operating assumptions through terminal value
Correctly handle sunk costs, opportunity costs, working capital, depreciation tax shield, and salvage value
Apply NPV, IRR, MIRR, and other investment criteria and explain any conflicts between them
Perform sensitivity analysis, scenario analysis, and break-even analysis to characterize project risk
Write a professional investment memorandum with clear recommendation and supporting analysis
deliverables: Excel pro-forma model with assumptions tab, operating forecast, depreciation schedule, cash flow summary, WACC derivation, and sensitivity analysis
NPV, IRR, MIRR, payback, discounted payback, and profitability index for the base case
Tornado chart showing sensitivity of NPV to each major input varied by +/-20%
Scenario analysis covering base, upside, downside, and stress cases with internally consistent input combinations
Break-even analysis for sales volume, unit price, and variable cost per unit
Investment memorandum (3 pages) addressed to the CFO with executive summary, analysis, risks, and clear recommendation

criteria: Cash flow model correctly distinguishes incremental from total cash flows and handles sunk costs and opportunity costs
Depreciation tax shield is computed correctly under the specified depreciation method (straight-line or MACRS)
Working capital changes are included as cash flows in the appropriate years and recovered at terminal value
WACC is derived from firm-specific inputs and applied consistently to discount cash flows
Excel model is professional with clear cell formatting, color coding of inputs vs calculations, and no hard-coded values in formulas
Tornado chart and scenario outputs are readable, properly labeled, and visually communicate risk effectively
Investment memorandum follows standard corporate finance formatting with executive summary and clear sections
Tables and figures have captions, source notes, and consistent rounding conventions
Sensitivity analysis correctly identifies the 2-3 inputs that most affect NPV and quantifies their impact
Scenario analysis presents internally consistent assumption sets (not mechanical uniform shifts)
Break-even analysis identifies the minimum performance required to justify the project
Conflicts between criteria (e.g., NPV vs IRR for mutually exclusive alternatives) are identified and resolved correctly
All assumptions in the model are sourced, justified, or explicitly flagged as estimates
Investment memorandum clearly articulates the recommendation with supporting analysis and risk discussion
Model structure is auditable with a third party able to trace any number to its source
Professional writing quality throughout with appropriate use of financial terminology
Complete incremental cash flow model with correct treatment of taxes, depreciation, and working capital
All investment criteria computed correctly with reconciliation when they conflict
Rigorous sensitivity and scenario analysis identifying project risk drivers
Professional investment memorandum with clear recommendation

major_assignments.title: Capital Structure and Payout Policy Case: Corporate Finance Decision Framework
major_assignments.description: Analyze a real corporate finance decision at a chosen public company involving capital structure changes (leverage increase or decrease, debt refinancing, new bond issuance, or leveraged recapitalization) and/or payout policy changes (dividend initiation, dividend increase, special dividend, or buyback program). Examples include Apple's capital return program, Microsoft's 2003 special dividend, Boeing's 2020 dividend suspension, Tesla's equity offerings, or Disney's Fox acquisition financing. Students must analyze the actual decision using the frameworks from weeks 9-15, compute the impact on WACC and firm value using Modigliani-Miller and trade-off theory, evaluate the decision from both pecking order and signaling perspectives, and assess whether the decision created or destroyed value. The deliverable is a 10-page case analysis report suitable for a corporate finance or M&A advisory team.
objectives: Apply MM, trade-off, and pecking order theories to a real corporate decision
Quantify the impact of leverage changes on WACC, cost of equity, and firm value
Analyze the market reaction to the decision and interpret the signal conveyed
Synthesize the capital budgeting, capital structure, and payout pillars of corporate finance into a coherent decision framework
deliverables: Case background (2 pages) describing the firm, the decision, the timing, and the market context
Quantitative analysis (3 pages) with pre-decision and post-decision WACC calculations, estimated tax shield value, and MM-based value impact
Theoretical analysis (2 pages) applying trade-off theory, pecking order theory, and signaling theory to the decision
Market reaction analysis (1 page) with stock price chart around the announcement date, return calculation, and interpretation of the market's reading
Synthesis and conclusion (2 pages) assessing whether the decision created or destroyed value and what the case teaches about real-world corporate finance
Excel worksheet with WACC calculations, tax shield valuation, stock price data, and all computations

criteria: WACC calculations are correct for both pre- and post-decision capital structures
Tax shield value is computed using the appropriate formula (T_c x D or present value of annual shields)
Levered and unlevered beta calculations are correct if leverage changes
Stock return calculations around the announcement date use proper methodology
Stock price charts are properly formatted with annotated announcement date and relevant time window
WACC comparison tables clearly present before-and-after values with explanations
Report follows professional case analysis formatting with clear structure and section headers
All figures and tables are captioned and sourced
Application of MM, trade-off, and pecking order theories is accurate and insightful
Analysis correctly interprets the signaling implications of the decision
Synthesis connects theory to observed market reaction with a defensible value assessment
Case analysis reveals a deeper understanding than a surface-level narrative
All facts and numbers are sourced from 10-K filings, press releases, or credible news sources
Writing is professional and clear, suitable for a corporate finance advisory team
Theoretical frameworks are referenced to specific textbook chapters or academic papers
The conclusion is defensible and acknowledges uncertainty where appropriate
Accurate quantitative analysis of WACC and firm value changes
Correct application of MM, trade-off, and pecking order theories
Insightful market reaction analysis with signaling interpretation
Synthesis that connects theory and practice with defensible conclusions

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes covering financial analysis, time value of money, bond and stock valuation, risk and return, CAPM, WACC, capital budgeting, capital structure, working capital, and payout policy

problem_sets.description: Weekly problem sets with quantitative exercises in Excel and on financial calculators covering every technical topic in the course

major_assignments.description: Three comprehensive assignments: full DCF and relative valuation report, capital budgeting case study, and capital structure and payout policy case analysis

midterm_examination.description: Written and quantitative exam covering weeks 1-8: financial analysis, time value of money, bond and stock valuation, risk and return, and CAPM

final_examination.description: Comprehensive final covering the full course with emphasis on weeks 9-15: WACC, capital budgeting, capital structure, working capital, and payout policy

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all lectures. Finance courses build cumulatively, and missing a session typically requires two sessions of catchup. More than 3 unexcused absences may reduce the participation component of the final grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day, with no submissions accepted more than 5 days past the deadline. Extensions granted only for documented emergencies, in writing, before the deadline.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of concepts and approaches is encouraged, but all submitted models, calculations, and writeups must be individual work. Collaboration on Excel model structure is permitted for problem sets; the major assignments are strictly individual.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must be your own. Using AI tools to generate large portions of written analysis or model structure without disclosure constitutes academic dishonesty. Sharing solutions with other students or using solutions from previous semesters is prohibited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students must have a financial calculator (TI BA II Plus or HP 10bII+) and access to Microsoft Excel with the Analysis ToolPak enabled. Access to Bloomberg Terminal, Capital IQ, or Refinitiv is helpful for the major assignments but not required; SEC EDGAR, Yahoo Finance, and Damodaran's NYU data pages are adequate substitutes and are free.
